Picture a simple “Running” (green) / “Stopped” (red) indicator pair fed off one relay: with a single-throw contact you can drive the green light OR the red light from that contact’s one output, but never both from the SAME contact — there is only one path out. To get “green when running, red when stopped” from one moving part, you need a DOUBLE-throw contact instead: one common terminal that routes to EITHER of two destinations depending on position (that’s the next module). Single-throw is not a restatement of NO/NC — it is the ceiling on what one contact can do, and it is exactly why real relays are built with multiple poles, and double-throw contacts, when a job needs to feed two destinations from one signal.

The contact itself is rated two ways: voltage and current. A typical small general-purpose relay might be rated 10A at 250VAC or 10A at 30VDC. Those are NOT the same rating — DC is harder to interrupt because there is no natural zero-crossing to kill the arc, so the DC rating is usually at a lower voltage. Blow past the contact's current rating even momentarily and you get an arc that pits the contact surface, depositing carbon and transfer metal between the faces. A pitted single-throw contact does not behave like an open gap — it behaves like a high-resistance partial connection. You will read voltage ACROSS the pitted contact (because there is a voltage drop), and the load may run weakly or intermittently. The diagnostic fingerprint is a measurable voltage drop across a contact that is supposed to be closed: more than about 0.5V across a healthy contact under load is a red flag; 5V across it means it's failing.

Single-throw contacts feeding an inductive load (solenoid coil, relay coil, motor) face an additional threat: the inductive kick when the circuit opens. A coil stores energy in its magnetic field and releases it as a voltage spike the instant the contact opens — commonly 5 to 10 times the supply voltage for an unprotected circuit. On a 24VDC circuit that can be a 200V+ spike that fires across the contact gap as an arc every single time the relay opens. Over thousands of cycles those arcs erode the single contact face you have, and they also generate radiated electrical noise that upsets nearby sensors and PLCs. The fix is a suppressor across the load, not across the contact: an RC snubber on AC inductive loads, a flyback diode on DC inductive loads wired cathode toward positive. Single-throw contacts live longer when the load they're switching is protected.

Because a single-throw contact is the simplest of switch topologies, it is also the easiest to misread when tracing a circuit under power. A single-throw NO contact that is stuck open due to mechanical binding or a broken spring will read full supply voltage across it — because all the supply voltage is dropped across the gap. A technician who has not thought through the circuit will see voltage at both terminals and conclude power is present when in fact the load sees nothing. The single-throw lesson here is directional: measure voltage across the contact (should be near zero when closed and conducting) rather than voltage to ground at each terminal separately. That one habit change will save you an hour of head-scratching when a mechanically open contact is pretending to be 'powered.'

failureModes.symptom: Machine runs weakly or heats up faster than normal, sometimes trips an overload that should not trip at that load level
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage ACROSS the closed contact under load — a healthy contact reads under 0.5V; a pitted or carbonized single-throw contact may read 3–8V, indicating high contact resistance burning watts and starving the load

failureModes.symptom: Single-throw contact chatter — load flickers rapidly or relay buzzes audibly instead of pulling in solid
failureModes.catch: Measure control voltage AT the coil terminals with the relay loaded: an open-circuit meter reads 24V but collapses to 8–12V when the coil tries to pull in, indicating a loose terminal or undersized wire dropping voltage under the coil's inrush current (typically 6–10x sealed current on a DC relay)

failureModes.symptom: Load stays on after the control signal removes power — single-throw contact will not open
failureModes.catch: With power removed and the coil confirmed de-energized (0V coil terminals), measure resistance across the output contact: a welded contact reads 0 ohms; a mechanically binding contact may read 0 ohms until you tap the relay body, at which point resistance jumps to open-circuit (OL)

failureModes.symptom: Load runs fine for hours then drops out momentarily — intermittent outages with no obvious cause, no fault code, circuit appears normal on inspection
failureModes.catch: Wiggle each terminal screw on the single-throw output circuit while the load is running — a loose screw-terminal connection will reproduce the dropout or cause a momentary voltage dip readable on a min/max-capturing DMM set to capture minimum DC volts

warStory: We had a packaging line conveyor that would trip its overload every afternoon around 2 PM with no explanation — ran fine all morning. The electrician before me replaced the overload relay twice. I finally put a clamp meter on the motor lead and a DMM across the contactor's single-throw pole feeding L2 and caught it: 7.4V drop across the contact. The contact face was so pitted it was essentially a resistor in series with the motor, adding heat to the winding and tripping the overload only after the ambient temperature in the panel rose during the afternoon shift. The root cause was that someone had been jogging the conveyor under load to clear jams for years — every jog is an inrush arc on that single contact. Twenty minutes to swap the contactor; zero trips since.
fieldNotes: A healthy closed contact under load should drop less than 0.5V across it — anything over 1V in a 24VDC circuit is worth investigating; over 3V means replace it now
DC contacts are rated lower than AC contacts of the same physical size because DC arcs do not self-extinguish at a zero-crossing — never substitute a DC load rating with an AC spec or vice versa

Inrush current on a relay coil can be 6–10x the sealed (holding) current — a 24VDC coil that draws 50mA holding may pull 300–500mA for the first 10–50ms; wire resistance and terminal resistance that look fine at sealed current cause chatter or no-pull-in under inrush

Single-throw contacts on motor starters are typically rated in horsepower at a given voltage, not just amps — a 10A contact rating does not automatically mean it can switch a 10A motor; check the horsepower rating on the starter nameplate

quickCheck.symptom: A 24VDC solenoid valve fed through a single-throw relay contact is firing sluggishly. The PLC output and relay coil both check out fine (steady 24V at the coil terminals when commanded on), and the valve does eventually open, but slower than the identical valve on the next station over. You notice the terminal block near the contactor's output pole feels slightly warm to the touch.

options.label: Measure voltage across the closed single-throw contact (its two terminals) while the valve is commanded on
options.why: This is the direct diagnostic: a healthy closed contact drops under 0.5V. A pitted or carbonized contact acts like a resistor in series with the load, showing several volts across it while it feeds the valve weakly and dissipates heat right at the terminal — matching the warmth you felt. This isolates the fault to the contact itself instead of guessing.

options.label: Replace the PLC output card, since a weak digital output would explain a slow-firing solenoid
options.why: The coil already reads a solid 24V when commanded, which rules out the PLC output and the coil circuit — the problem shows up downstream of the coil, at the load side. Swapping the output card ignores evidence you already have and won't fix a resistive contact.

options.label: Increase the PLC output pulse duration so the valve has more time to fully open
options.why: This treats the symptom, not the cause. If the contact is developing resistance, it will keep heating up and eventually fail outright (or start intermittently dropping the load); masking a weak signal with a longer pulse just delays a wider contactor failure and burns more heat every cycle.

title: Double Throw: Two Destinations (the Selector)
objective: Define Double Throw as one common contact that switches between two outputs, never both at once.
plainExplanation: DOUBLE THROW means the drawbridge can swing to EITHER of two destinations. The contact has a shared input (the 'common') and it connects to output A or output B — one is open while the other is closed, never both. This is a selector. The first useful example: one double-throw contact drives a 'Stopped' light and a 'Running' light, so exactly one is always lit. Think of a three-way hallway switch: the same common picks between two paths.
interactiveElement: A double-throw contact with a common and two outputs (Stopped bulb / Running bulb); throwing it visibly swings the bar from one to the other so one bulb always lights and the other always goes dark.
checkQuestion: On a double-throw contact, can BOTH outputs be connected at the same time?
checkAnswer: No. A double-throw contact connects to one output OR the other, never both. As one closes, the other opens — that's what makes it a selector between two destinations.
buildsOn: m06-single-throw-one-destination (add a second destination — original print 6's Stopped/Running pair)
deepDive: A double-throw contact isn't just an abstract selector — it's a single moving bridge (the wiper or armature) that makes one connection by pressure and the other by spring return. The contact that closes when the coil is energized is the NO side (normal operation); the one that closes at rest is the NC side. On a real DPDT relay, the common terminal is the pivot point. When you energize a 24VDC coil — which pulls typically 50-150mA sealed after an inrush spike of 3-6x that value — the armature snaps from the NC contact to the NO contact in under 10 milliseconds. That snap matters: a sluggish or chattering coil (low voltage, usually below 85% of rated) will bounce the wiper between both contacts, arcing across both output terminals before it settles. In a Stopped/Running indicator circuit, that chatter shows up as both lights flickering simultaneously — a condition the circuit is supposed to make impossible, but dirty control power makes it happen.

Contact ratings are where Beginner-level circuits meet hard reality. A typical DPDT relay — say an Omron G2R or Potter & Brumfield KRP series — is rated for 10A at 250VAC or 30VDC on its contacts. That rating is for resistive loads. Inductive loads (solenoids, small motors, contactor coils) get derated by roughly 30-50% because they generate a back-EMF voltage spike at the moment of contact opening, which tries to sustain the arc. A 24VDC pilot light rated at 20mA is nowhere near the contact limit — but swap that lamp for a solenoid valve coil and you're now arcing across the NC contact every time the relay releases. Over hundreds of cycles, the NC side pits and the NO side accumulates transferred metal. Eventually the NC terminal won't make a reliable connection anymore. The Stopped light starts flickering even when the machine is idle, not because the lamp is bad — because the NC contact is pitted and bouncing. This is the most common way double-throw contacts degrade in the field and it's almost never diagnosed correctly the first time.

The other failure mode unique to double-throw contacts is mechanical binding — the wiper sticks in one position. This can be from contamination (oil mist or dust in environments without sealed relays), excessive spring fatigue from thermal cycling, or a contact that welded from an overcurrent event. A welded NO contact is the worst case: the relay now behaves like a single-throw stuck closed. In a Stopped/Running indicator circuit, the machine shuts down but the Running light never goes off because the DT contact is frozen on the NO side. Operators call the machine dead but the panel says it's running. That mismatch is the symptom — and if maintenance doesn't understand double-throw mechanics, they'll chase a control circuit fault that doesn't exist. The actual test is to remove power from the coil completely and measure continuity: common-to-NC should be near zero ohms, common-to-NO should be open. If you get near-zero on both, the armature is welded or mechanically stuck and the relay must be replaced.

One subtlety worth understanding now is why the double-throw selector is preferred for status indication over two separate single-throw contacts wired to the same logic. If you use two separate NO contacts — one relay for Running, one for Stopped — there's a window, however brief, where both contacts are open simultaneously during transition. Both lights go dark for a fraction of a second. In an automated sequence with fast timers, that dead window can trigger upstream PLC inputs reading a false de-energized state. The DT contact eliminates the dead window by design: NC opens at the exact instant NO closes (zero gap by contact geometry). That makes the DT a make-before-break or break-before-make contact by mechanical specification — check the datasheet. Most industrial DT contacts are break-before-make (the old connection releases before the new one makes), which gives exactly one state at all times. This is the engineering reason double-throw exists, not just convenience.

failureModes.symptom: Both Stopped and Running lights flicker on and off simultaneously while the machine is supposed to be in a stable state
failureModes.catch: Measure AC voltage at the coil terminals with a true-RMS meter while symptoms occur — a coil rated 24VDC reading 18-20VDC or an AC ripple on the DC supply above 2V peak-to-peak means insufficient hold voltage is causing chatter; the DT wiper is bouncing between both contacts

failureModes.symptom: Running light stays lit even after Stop is pressed and the motor has stopped; machine is confirmed dead but panel shows Running
failureModes.catch: Remove all power to the control circuit, then measure resistance from common terminal to NC terminal and common to NO terminal — a welded or stuck contact shows near-zero ohms on BOTH instead of continuity on NC only and open on NO; replace the relay

failureModes.symptom: Stopped light flickers intermittently even though nothing in the circuit is changing; starts happening gradually over weeks
failureModes.catch: With coil de-energized (relay at rest), probe common-to-NC with a milliohm measurement or a 9V continuity buzzer under load — pitted NC contact shows varying resistance from 0.1 to several ohms instead of a stable near-zero; contact surface has degraded from repeated inductive arc on the NC side

failureModes.symptom: After a new relay is installed the Stopped and Running lights behave backwards — Running light on when machine is stopped and vice versa
failureModes.catch: Check terminal assignment on the replacement relay: on an 8-pin DPDT (e.g., Omron G2R-2), the NO and NC contacts swap pin positions between manufacturers and even between relay families; confirm pin-out against the datasheet, not the old relay's wiring

warStory: We had a film-line laminator at the Greenfield plant that showed Running on the HMI annunciator panel even when the line was locked out for a belt change — maintenance had learned to ignore it because 'the panel always lies on that machine.' Turned out the original installer had wired both the Stopped and Running lamps to the NO side of two separate single-throw relay contacts instead of using the DT contact, and one relay had welded shut from a brief short on the 120VAC lamp circuit three years earlier. Nobody ever traced it because the symptom looked like a wiring mistake, not a failed contact. Two hours of head-scratching until a continuity check on the de-energized relay showed zero ohms on NO — same as NC — and the burnt contact face was visible with a flashlight once we pulled the relay from the socket.
fieldNotes: A typical DPDT plug-in relay (Omron G2R-2, Potter & Brumfield KRP) is rated 10A resistive / 5A inductive at 250VAC — always derate by at least 30% for inductive loads like solenoid coils or contactor coils on the output
Coil pull-in voltage is typically 75-85% of rated; hold (sealed) voltage drops to 10-15% of rated — a coil rated 24VDC will chatter if control voltage sags below about 18-20V under load
Break-before-make DT contacts guarantee only ONE output is live at any instant — verify 'BBM' on the datasheet if simultaneous output overlap would cause a safety or sequence problem in your circuit
24VDC pilot lamps draw 20-50mA — well within contact ratings — but a sneak path through a burned lamp (short-circuit) can exceed the contact's interrupt rating; always fuse each output leg separately

quickCheck.symptom: A machine is shut down and locked out, but its 'Running' indicator light on the panel is still on. You have confirmed 0V at the relay coil.

options.label: The indicator light is getting phantom voltage from another circuit.
options.why: Phantom voltage from capacitive coupling can cause a faint glow in an LED, but rarely illuminates an incandescent bulb fully, and can be disproven with a low-impedance meter.

options.label: The PLC output card for the light is stuck on.
options.why: The fault lies between the PLC and the light. The relay is the component that translates the PLC signal; if its coil is de-energized, the problem is in the relay or its wiring.

options.label: The relay's double-throw contact has welded on the Normally-Open side.
options.why: This is the classic failure mode. With the coil de-energized, the contact should have returned to the NC position. If the NO side still has continuity, it is physically stuck or welded.

title: What Is a Pole: One Independent Switch Inside
objective: Define a pole in plain words — a single input contact point that supplies a load — and connect it to throws.
plainExplanation: A POLE is the trade's term for one complete, independent switch built inside the relay — one input contact point that supplies electricity to a load. A pole can be single-throw (one destination) or double-throw (two destinations). Picture poles as separate lanes that all flip together when the relay acts, but each lane carries its own circuit. One pole = one circuit you can switch; we'll soon stack several poles in one relay to control several circuits at once.
interactiveElement: A single pole shown as a labeled lane (input dot → contact bar → output dot[s]); a toggle lets the learner make that one pole single-throw or double-throw and watch the output dots change from one to two.
checkQuestion: In plain words, what is a 'pole'?
checkAnswer: A pole is a single input contact point that supplies electricity to a load — one independent switch inside the relay. Each pole carries its own circuit and can be single-throw or double-throw.
buildsOn: m07-double-throw-two-destinations (a pole is the switch that can be single- or double-throw — original print 2)
deepDive: Each pole in a relay is a physically independent switch, but they all ride the same armature — that one metal bar the electromagnet pulls. This is the whole mechanical point: one coil actuation, multiple switched circuits, perfectly synchronized. In a DPDT relay (8 pins, two poles, two throws each), Pole 1 and Pole 2 flip at exactly the same instant because they're both riveted to the same moving bar. That synchronization is load-bearing in real applications — if you need a 'Stopped' lamp to go dark at the exact moment a 'Running' lamp lights, they must share one armature, not two separate relays with their own coil delays.

Here's where poles get you into trouble if you treat them like they're all the same: each pole has its own contact surfaces, its own spring tension, its own wear history. A relay rated '10A at 240VAC per pole' means per-pole — that's not 10A total shared across all poles, it's 10A per independent switching lane. Wire two poles in parallel to carry 20A and you're playing with fire, literally. The current doesn't distribute evenly: one contact always closes a few microseconds before the other due to armature tilt or uneven contact wear. The first pole to close takes the full 20A inrush; if the arc it draws is heavy enough, it can weld that contact before the second pole even touches. Now you have one welded pole and one floating, and your 'redundant' wiring is running 20A through one 10A contact — you find this at 3 AM when the motor won't stop.

The separate-lane nature of poles is also what makes sneak paths possible in multi-pole circuits. If you wire both poles of a DPST to the same load from opposite ends — thinking you're adding redundancy — you can create a loop where the load is energized from a direction your fuse doesn't protect. Control-transformer secondary grounds make this worse: if your 24VDC supply has a grounded negative rail and you use both poles of a relay to switch the positive leg at two different points, a ground fault anywhere in the wiring between those poles puts unexpected voltage on a circuit you thought was switched off. The discipline: treat each pole as its own separate, complete circuit. Route power in one end, load out the other, never use two poles as a return path for each other.

A less obvious but real field consequence: armature tilt under uneven contact loading. If Pole 1 is carrying a resistive load (easy, low inrush) and Pole 2 is switching an inductive motor load with 6x inrush, the armature sees asymmetric force during pick-up. Over thousands of cycles the pole under heavy arc stress develops pitting and contact resistance while the easy pole stays relatively clean. When you measure across the worn pole under load you might see 3-4V drop where there should be less than 0.5V — that drop means heat, and heat means accelerated contact degradation. The fix isn't just replacing the relay; it's figuring out why one pole is doing all the hard work and whether the loads should be rebalanced across relays.

failureModes.symptom: One controlled circuit stays on (or off) after the relay is de-energized, while the other pole switches normally
failureModes.catch: Measure resistance across the stuck pole's contacts with the relay de-energized: if you read less than 1 ohm when it should be open (infinite), the contact is welded. You can sometimes hear it — the relay clicks but the armature doesn't fully return because the weld is holding one pole fast. Compare with resistance across the good pole (should be open/OL).

failureModes.symptom: Load on one pole of a multi-pole relay runs hotter than expected; breaker nuisance-trips on that circuit but not others fed by the same relay
failureModes.catch: With relay energized and load running, measure voltage drop across each pole from input terminal to output terminal. A healthy contact drops under 0.3V at rated current. A pitted or high-resistance contact on one pole will drop 2-5V at the same current — that drop times the current is real watts burned at the contact. This is the reading that tells you which pole to blame before you pull the whole relay.

failureModes.symptom: Machine logic behaves as if two separate events are happening at slightly different times — e.g., a 'Stopped' lamp flickers for a half-second before the 'Running' lamp comes on, every cycle
failureModes.catch: This is a bent or worn armature causing sequential rather than simultaneous contact closure. With a clamp meter on each pole's circuit, you'll see the current onset is staggered by 20-50ms. On a relay that should close all poles simultaneously, any measurable stagger between poles means the armature is damaged or the relay base is cracked. Replace the relay — the mechanical geometry is gone and it will get worse.

failureModes.symptom: Two poles wired in parallel to a single high-current load: load runs but the relay runs hot and one pole shows arc pitting far worse than the other after several weeks
failureModes.catch: Measure DC resistance of each pole's contacts separately with the relay energized (use a milliohm meter or the diode/low-ohm function on a quality Fluke). The pole with pitting reads 0.5-2 ohms; the other reads under 0.05 ohms. This proves one pole is carrying the lion's share of current. The cure is not a new relay — it's de-rating the load or using a properly rated single-pole relay, not parallel poles.

warStory: We had a 4-pole motor starter on a large mixer — three poles for 3-phase and the fourth wired as a seal-in. The mixer started acting possessed: it would run fine for a shift, then randomly fail to seal in and drop out on release of the Start button. I went straight to the seal-in pole because that's what the symptom said, checked the contact, checked the wire — everything looked clean. Took me an embarrassingly long time to find it: the armature pivot pin had worn just enough that under load on the three power poles, the armature flexed slightly and the fourth, lighter-duty seal-in pole was the last to close — just barely, intermittently. The fix was a new starter, but the lesson was that poles share one mechanical body, and if that body is worn, not every pole is created equal anymore.
fieldNotes: Each pole's contact rating is INDEPENDENT — a 10A/240VAC DPST relay is 10A per pole, not 10A shared. Never parallel poles to get more amperage.
A healthy contact drops less than 0.3V at rated current. More than 1V across a closed contact under load means pitting or oxidation — it will get worse.

All poles on one relay share one armature, so they ALL flip at once or none do — this synchronization is exactly why we use multi-pole relays instead of separate single-pole relays when timing between circuits matters.
DPDT = 8 pins (2 coil + 2 commons + 4 outputs). If you count 8 pins on an unmarked relay, you're holding a DPDT — use that to orient yourself before you wire anything.

quickCheck.symptom: You walk up to a panel where a DPDT relay drives a 'Running' lamp on Pole 1 and a 'Stopped' lamp on Pole 2. Normally they switch in perfect sync, but lately you notice the 'Stopped' lamp stays lit for almost a full second after the 'Running' lamp comes on, every single start-up cycle. Both lamps and their wiring check out fine individually. What do you conclude?

options.label: One of the two lamps is simply slower to warm up than the other, so this is a normal lamp characteristic and not a relay problem
options.why: Incandescent/LED lamp warm-up differences are near-instant (milliseconds), not a full second, and a lamp fault wouldn't produce a consistent, repeatable stagger tied to the switching event itself — this points to the switching mechanism, not the lamp.

options.label: The relay's armature or base is worn/damaged, causing the poles to close sequentially instead of simultaneously — the relay needs to be replaced
options.why: Both poles ride the same armature and are supposed to flip in the same instant. A measurable, repeatable stagger between poles (here, visible as almost a full second of overlap) means the mechanical geometry that keeps them synchronized is gone — a bent or worn armature or a cracked base. That's a mechanical failure that only gets worse, so replace the relay rather than chasing the lamps or wiring.

options.label: The coil is being under-driven, so give it a few more cycles to fully seat and the poles will re-sync on their own
options.why: A weak coil pick-up would affect when the whole armature moves, not create a lasting timing gap between two poles that share that same armature — and 'a few more cycles' won't fix a mechanical wear problem that will only progress, not self-correct.

title: Putting It Together: SPST, SPDT, DPST, DPDT
objective: Decode relay names by combining pole count (S/D) with throw count (ST/DT) into one consistent naming scheme.
plainExplanation: Now the alphabet soup makes sense: the first letters are the number of POLES (Single or Double) and the last letters are the number of THROWS (Single or Double). SPST = Single Pole Single Throw (one switch, one destination — a basic on/off). SPDT = Single Pole Double Throw (one switch, two destinations — the Stopped/Running selector). DPST = two independent on/off switches that flip together. DPDT = two selectors that flip together. Read any relay name left-to-right: how many lanes, then how many destinations each lane has.
interactiveElement: A 2x2 builder: learner clicks 'poles: 1 or 2' and 'throws: 1 or 2' and the diagram redraws live, auto-printing the correct name (SPST/SPDT/DPST/DPDT) with each pole and throw highlighted.
checkQuestion: Decode SPDT: how many poles, how many throws, and what's a real use?
checkAnswer: Single Pole (one switch) Double Throw (two destinations). It selects between two outputs — exactly the Stopped-light / Running-light circuit where one is always lit.
buildsOn: m08-what-is-a-pole (combine pole count with throw count into the standard naming — original prints 2 & 5)
deepDive: The four-letter code is a blueprint, not jargon. Unpack it once and you'll read any relay off a nameplate for the rest of your career. The first letter (S or D) counts independent switch lanes — the POLES. The second letter (P) is just the word 'pole' spelled out. The third letter (S or D) counts destinations per lane — the THROWS. The fourth letter (T) is just 'throw.' So DPDT = Double Pole Double Throw = two independent lanes, each swinging between two destinations. Put a DPDT in front of you: every throw of the actuator moves BOTH lanes simultaneously, and within each lane, closing one destination forces the other open. That mechanical linkage is the key — poles are ganged, throws are exclusive.

Where does each type actually live in the plant? SPST is your workhorse discrete output: one contact feeding one load — a pilot light, a solenoid valve, an alarm buzzer. It's the building block. SPDT is the selector, the Stopped/Running pair: one common, two outputs, exactly one lit at all times — no ambiguity, no intermediate state where both could be dark or both lit. That exclusivity matters for safety interlocks. DPST doubles the switched circuits without doubling the wiring back to your PLC output: one coil command throws two physically separate circuits together — common in transformer primary switching where you need both legs of a 240VAC single-phase circuit opened simultaneously. DPDT is the Swiss Army knife: interlocked forward/reverse contactors use DPDT auxiliary contacts to physically prevent both coils from energizing at once — one DPDT configured so each pole's NC contact is wired into the opposite coil's control path.

Contact ratings are where the naming stops being abstract and starts costing you money. A DPDT relay rated 10A/250VAC sounds robust — two poles, two throws, must be versatile. But that 10A is continuous resistive current. Inductive loads — motors, solenoids, transformer primaries — generate a back-EMF spike when switched off, and they draw 6–10x nameplate current on closure. An SPST contact rated 10A resistive may only be rated 2–3A for inductive loads, and that number drops further at 480VAC versus 120VAC (higher voltage means the arc at contact separation carries more energy and has to stretch farther to extinguish). Exceed the inductive rating on a DPST or DPDT and you get contact welding — the arc melts the silver-cadmium oxide face of one contact into the other. The pole is now mechanically stuck closed even after the coil de-energizes. The symptom: motor runs even with the relay coil dead-measured at 0V. The catch: force the contacts open by hand with power off and you hear — or feel — them pull apart. Replace the relay; those faces don't recover.

On a DPDT, never assume both poles are identical just because they're stamped the same rating. After years of service one pole may carry a hard inductive load (a motor starter coil, 40–80mA inrush) and the other may carry a light resistive load (a 5mA signal to a PLC input). The heavy-side pole will pit and carbon long before the light-side pole shows wear. When a DPDT acts intermittently — machine drops out randomly but the coil voltage stays solid — put your meter on each pole's output individually and cycle the circuit 20 times. You'll often find the loaded pole reads 23–24V clean on 18 out of 20 cycles and drops to 14–18V on 2 of them, while the light pole reads perfect every time. That partial-contact resistance under load is classic pitting: the pole appears fine on a static continuity check (milliohms) but under 80–100mA it's dropping several volts. This is why the half-split method must go to live-loaded measurements, not just ohmmeter checks with the relay de-energized.

failureModes.symptom: Machine runs continuously and won't stop — relay coil measures 0VDC (confirmed dead) but the load stays powered
failureModes.catch: With the circuit de-energized, try to separate the contacts by hand (insulated tool, LOTO first). On a DPDT or DPST, welded contacts feel sticky or audibly snap apart. Confirm with a low-Z ohmmeter across the suspect pole: welded contacts read under 1 ohm with no coil voltage. Replace the relay; check whether the load's inductive surge exceeded the contact rating.

failureModes.symptom: On a DPST application, one circuit (e.g., the load) energizes normally but the second circuit (e.g., the seal-in or indicator) is dead even though both poles should throw together
failureModes.catch: Measure across the dead pole output with the relay energized. If the coil is pulled in (armature seated, you can hear/feel the click) and you read supply voltage across the open output terminals instead of near-zero, that pole is open — burned, pitted through, or the contact bridge cracked. Static ohm check with relay energized via a test bench: should read under 0.5 ohm; open pole reads infinite. Swap the relay.

failureModes.symptom: SPDT selector circuit: both output indicators show faint glow simultaneously instead of one-on/one-off — ghost voltage condition
failureModes.catch: Switch meter to AC millivolts or use a low-impedance voltmeter (Fluke 28II or similar with LoZ mode). Ghost voltage from capacitive coupling in a long parallel cable run reads 30–80V on a standard high-impedance DMM but collapses to under 2V on a low-Z meter. No current can drive a real load at that level. If both indicators glow because the SPDT common is leaking through a sneak path (parallel wiring), disconnect one output wire at the terminal and see which indicator is the real one and which is the ghost.

failureModes.symptom: DPDT interlock for forward/reverse motor control: pressing Reverse fires Forward instead, or both contactors try to pull in simultaneously (loud clunk, possible blown fuse)
failureModes.catch: On a DPDT used as a mechanical interlock, each pole's NC terminal is wired into the opposite coil's circuit. Check the wiring against the schematic — verify the NC terminals (not NO) are in use for the interlock path. With the relay de-energized, put the ohmmeter from common to NC terminal: should be 0 ohm. From common to NO terminal: infinite. A miswired relay with both interlock contacts as NO provides no interlocking — both coils can pull in simultaneously. This is a wiring error, not a component fault, but the result is catastrophic on a motor reversing circuit.

failureModes.symptom: SPST relay intermittently drops the load under normal operation — coil voltage is solid, no vibration apparent, temperature normal
failureModes.catch: Inspect the terminal screws on both the coil side and the contact side with the circuit de-energized. Torque spec for most 300mA–10A relay sockets is 0.5–0.8 N·m (4–7 in-lb). A loose coil terminal that makes intermittent contact doesn't read on a static voltage check — the probe pressure holds it. Use a milli-ohm test (Kelvin clip, 200mA test current) across each terminal connection, not just the contact. Resistance above 0.5 ohm at a terminal junction under load will drop 50–100mV at coil current, which on a marginal voltage supply (24V supply already at 21V) can pull the coil below its drop-out threshold (typically 60–70% of rated voltage, or about 14–17VDC on a 24V coil).

warStory: We had a DPDT relay on a drum-indexer at a plastics plant — one pole ran the advance solenoid, the other pole fed the 'at-position' signal back to the PLC. Machine was randomly faulting 'position not confirmed' three or four times a shift, and every time maintenance got there the relay ohm-tested fine. Took three weeks before somebody actually clamped an amp meter on the position-signal pole under load: it was dropping to 17V at the contact under the 60mA PLC input current draw, even though the coil was pulled tight and the advance solenoid on the other pole was reading a clean 23.8V. The position-signal pole had been carrying a PLC 24V input card that had a snubber cap on it for years — the tiny capacitive inrush every scan-cycle had pitted that one pole face while the other stayed pristine. Swapped the relay in ten minutes; it never faulted again. The lesson: two poles on the same DPDT do not age at the same rate.
fieldNotes: DPDT pin count = 8: 2 coil + 3 per pole (1 common + 1 NO + 1 NC) × 2 poles. Memorize 8-pin = DPDT; it's the most common relay you'll pull off a shelf.
Contact ratings always derate for inductive loads — expect 25–40% of the resistive amp rating for motor/solenoid loads. A '10A' DPDT relay may be only 2.5A rated for inductive DC loads.

SPDT common terminal is the input; NC terminal is the output when coil is de-energized; NO terminal is the output when coil is energized. Never reverse this — wiring NC where NO is expected gives you inverted logic that's almost impossible to trace on a dark machine.

Drop-out voltage on most 24VDC coils is 60–70% of rated voltage (≈14–17V). A coil chattering or intermittently dropping on a '24V' circuit often means your supply is sagging to 19–21V under load — not a relay fault, a supply or wiring fault.

quickCheck.symptom: On a motor reversing circuit using a DPDT relay for the interlock, pressing FORWARD works. Pressing REVERSE immediately trips the main breaker with a loud bang.

options.label: The REVERSE motor contactor coil is shorted.
options.why: A shorted coil would likely blow the control circuit fuse, not the main 3-phase motor breaker. The symptom points to a phase-to-phase short.

options.label: The DPDT interlock is wired to the NO contacts instead of the NC contacts.
options.why: This is the critical error. The interlock relies on the NC contacts to prevent the opposing contactor from energizing. Wiring to the NO contacts provides no interlock, allowing both FWD and REV contactors to pull in, creating a direct short across the power lines.

options.label: The DPDT relay has a welded contact.
options.why: A welded contact would likely cause one direction to be stuck on, but it wouldn't necessarily cause an immediate dead short the moment the other direction is commanded.

title: The Coil: an Electromagnet That Pulls the Contacts
objective: Explain that a relay is contacts PLUS a coil, and energizing the coil mechanically flips every pole at once.
plainExplanation: Here's the magic that turns a plain switch into a RELAY: a coil of wire that becomes an electromagnet when you energize it. Send voltage to the coil and it pulls a metal bar, snapping every pole over at the same instant — all the NO contacts close and all the NC contacts open. Cut the coil power and a spring snaps everything back to normal. So a relay lets a small safe control signal (the coil) throw contacts that switch a totally separate, bigger circuit (the load). That separation is the whole point.
interactiveElement: An exploded relay: a wire from the supply to the coil; clicking 'energize coil' animates the electromagnet pulling the contact bar so NO closes and NC opens together; de-energize and the spring returns them. Coil shown labeled 24VDC.
checkQuestion: What physically happens to the contacts when you energize the coil, and what happens when you remove power?
checkAnswer: Energizing the coil makes an electromagnet that pulls the bar, closing all NO contacts and opening all NC contacts at once. Remove power and a spring snaps them back to their normal resting state.
buildsOn: m09-naming-spst-spdt-dpst-dpdt (the poles we named are now thrown by a coil — this makes it a relay, original print 7)
deepDive: A relay coil is just resistance and inductance in series — wire wound around an iron core. When you apply rated voltage, current climbs through that resistance and the resulting magnetic flux pulls the armature down. Here's the number that surprises most newcomers: inrush current on a small AC relay coil hits 6-10x the sealed (holding) current in the first few milliseconds. A 120VAC coil that draws 60 mA sealed will spike to 400-600 mA at pull-in. That inrush is not a problem on a clean supply — the coil is designed for it. But it becomes a problem when the control voltage is marginal. If the supply voltage sags under load and the coil can't develop enough magnetic force to pull the armature fully seated, the air gap stays large, inductance stays low, current stays high, and the coil sits in a continuous inrush condition. At 400-600 mA of sustained draw in a coil designed to carry 60 mA, the wire insulation fails in minutes. The symptom: relay hums, gets hot, eventually the coil burns open and the relay never picks up again. The cause everyone misses: the control supply was 98V at idle but 82V under load — below the 85% minimum pull-in voltage most coils require.

DC coils behave differently from AC coils and they fail differently. A DC coil has no inductance-dominated inrush in the AC sense — instead, current climbs as E/R until the magnetic field is established. A 24VDC coil rated at 750 ohms draws I = 24/750 = 32 mA steady-state, which is why 24VDC control is so gentle on wiring. But the DC coil stores energy in that magnetic field, and when you interrupt power the collapsing field generates a voltage spike that can easily reach 10-20x the supply voltage — 240-480V on a 24VDC coil — in the microsecond it takes to collapse. That spike kills PLC transistor outputs and destroys solid-state devices in the same panel. The fix is a flyback diode (snubber) across the coil, and if the relay you're ordering doesn't have one built in, you add it externally. A lot of nuisance output-card failures in PLC panels trace straight back to a relay coil without suppression.

The coil is also the first thing to check when a relay won't pull in, but there's a wrong way and a right way to test it. The wrong way: disconnect the coil leads and ring it with an ohmmeter, get a valid reading, declare the coil good. An open coil will read OL — catch. But a coil that reads correct DC resistance can still fail to pull in because the issue isn't the coil at all — it's voltage drop in the control wiring. A meter reads 24V at the supply terminals. You walk to the coil terminals and read 24V there too — with the coil disconnected. Reconnect the coil and measure again: now you might see 14V because the wire run has enough resistance that under the coil's load current, the drop is significant. V = I × R, and even 5 ohms of wiring resistance in a 32 mA circuit drops 0.16V — trivial. But in a corroded terminal that's developed 50 ohms of contact resistance, the drop is 1.6V. String four loose terminals together and you lose 6-8V before the coil even sees it. Always measure voltage at the coil terminals under load, not at the supply.

The mechanical side of the coil-and-armature system has its own failure mode: a relay that chatters or buzzes on an AC supply almost always means the shading ring — a small copper ring embedded in the iron core face — is cracked or missing. On an AC relay the magnetic flux crosses zero twice per cycle (120 times per second on 60 Hz), which means the attractive force goes to zero 120 times per second. Without the shading ring to create a phase-shifted secondary flux that holds the armature during those zero crossings, the armature bounces at 120 Hz. The buzz you hear is mechanical. The consequence is severe: each bounce is a partial arc across the main contacts, and contact material transfers and pits on every half-cycle. A relay that chatters for 30 minutes can pit its contacts enough that it won't pass full rated current even when it eventually seats. On DC relays there are no zero crossings so no shading ring is needed — this is another reason modern controls moved to 24VDC.

failureModes.symptom: Relay hums, runs warm, eventually coil goes open and relay won't pick up at all
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage AT the coil terminals with coil connected and energized — if you see less than 85% of rated (e.g., below 20.4V on a 24VDC coil), the supply is sagging under load. Meter at the panel supply reads nominal; meter at coil terminals shows the truth.

failureModes.symptom: PLC discrete output card fails or gives erratic outputs; nearby relays work fine
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage spike on relay coil terminals with a scope at coil disconnect — DC relay without suppression will spike 200-500V on a 24VDC circuit. Check for flyback diode on coil or snubber across output terminals. No scope? Swap the suspect relay for one with a built-in suppressor and see if output card faults disappear.

failureModes.symptom: Relay buzzes or chatters continuously on an AC control circuit; contacts pit and erode rapidly
failureModes.catch: Remove the relay and inspect the pole face of the electromagnet under magnification — look for the shading ring (small copper or aluminum ring in a slot on the core face). A cracked or missing shading ring is the direct cause on AC. On DC supply, chatter means the control voltage is actually borderline AC ripple or the supply is failing.

failureModes.symptom: Relay coil measures correct resistance with ohmmeter but relay never pulls in
failureModes.catch: Energize the circuit and measure DC resistance of the wiring run itself: disconnect the coil, apply control voltage, and clip a milliammeter in series. Calculate expected current (E/R); if actual current is well below calculated, you have excessive wiring resistance. Corroded terminals read 50-200 ohms each where they should be under 0.1 ohm.

failureModes.symptom: Relay pulls in fine when cold, drops out intermittently after 30-60 minutes of operation
failureModes.catch: This is a thermally intermittent coil winding. Cold resistance reads normal. After thermal soak, one winding layer has a micro-crack that expands open with heat. Measure coil resistance with the panel at operating temperature — resistance will read high or OL. Replace the relay; there is no repair for a heat-cracked coil.

warStory: We had a hydraulic press that kept burning relay coils — third one in six weeks, same terminal block, same relay socket, operators swearing they hadn't changed anything. I finally caught it by leaving a meter on the coil terminals through a full production run: supply read 24.2V at the transformer; coil terminals read 19.1V at pull-in. Traced it to a single crimp terminal in the homerun that had been overtightened years ago, cracked the ferrule, and had been developing resistance slowly ever since. That one terminal was dropping 5V under the 32 mA coil load — barely enough to pull in, way too low to seat fully, so the coil ran in partial-inrush for its entire duty cycle and cooked. Swapped the terminal, measured 23.8V at the coil under load, never burned another coil on that press.
fieldNotes: AC relay coil pull-in requires minimum 85% of rated voltage; below that it may hum and stay unseated, drawing inrush current continuously until the coil burns open.

AC coil inrush current is typically 6-10x sealed current for the first 20-50 ms; a coil rated at 60 mA sealed can spike to 400+ mA at pick-up — normal and designed-in, but lethal if the coil never fully seats.

A 24VDC coil without flyback suppression generates a collapse spike of 10-20x supply voltage (240-480V) when de-energized — always spec a suppressed coil or add a 1N4007 diode externally across the coil terminals (cathode to positive).
Chatter on an AC relay = inspect the shading ring on the core face first; chatter on a DC relay = measure for AC ripple on the supply or marginal control voltage.

quickCheck.symptom: A 24VDC relay will not pull in. You disconnect its wires and measure 24.1V at the ends of the wires. As soon as you reconnect them to the coil terminals, the voltage reads 17.5V and the relay just hums.

options.label: The relay coil is internally shorted.
options.why: A shorted coil would draw extreme current, causing the power supply to trip or the voltage to drop to near zero, not settle at 17.5V.

options.label: The 24VDC power supply is failing.
options.why: While possible, the first measurement of 24.1V at the disconnected wires suggests the supply is fine under no load. The problem appears when current is drawn.

options.label: There is high resistance in the wiring or a terminal connection.
options.why: This is the classic 'voltage drop under load' scenario. A corroded terminal or bad crimp creates resistance that only reveals itself when the coil draws current, dropping several volts and starving the coil.

title: Add a Start Button — and Hit the Problem
objective: Wire a momentary Start button to the coil and discover why momentary control alone can't hold a machine on.
plainExplanation: Let's make the Stopped/Running selector actually controllable. We add a momentary START push button in the wire feeding the coil. Press it: coil energizes, contacts flip, Running light comes on, Stopped goes off — exactly what we want. But the instant you let go, the spring snaps everything back: Running off, Stopped on. The machine only runs while your finger holds the button. That's useless for a motor you need to run all day, and it's the exact problem the next pieces will solve.
interactiveElement: The SPDT circuit with a press-and-hold Start button: while held, Running lights; on release it springs back. A note tracks 'Coil energized: only while held' to make the gap obvious.
checkQuestion: With only a momentary Start button wired to the coil, what happens the moment you release the button?
checkAnswer: Everything springs back to normal — the coil de-energizes, Running goes off and Stopped comes back on. A momentary button can't hold the machine on; we need a way to keep the coil powered after release.
buildsOn: m10-the-coil-electromagnet (now we energize that coil with a real button — original print 7's problem)
deepDive: A 24VDC coil doesn't just sit there patiently waiting for current — it punches hard when first energized. That initial inrush can be 6 to 10 times the sealed holding current. A typical ice-cube relay coil that holds on 20mA will slam in on 120-180mA for a few milliseconds. When you wire a bare momentary button in series with that coil, all of that inrush current flows through the button's contacts on every press. A push button rated for 10A resistive is not necessarily happy switching an inductive load at startup — the collapsing magnetic field when you release generates a back-EMF spike that can arc the contacts, pitting them over hundreds of cycles. This is exactly why 24VDC control circuits almost always put a flyback diode or MOV across the coil: without it, that spike is real voltage — sometimes 3x to 5x the supply rail — and it's punishing the very button contacts you're counting on.

The voltage picture at the coil terminals is not static. With a good supply and a short wire run you'll read 24.0V across the coil while it's sealed. But press that button on a long wire run — say 50 feet of 18AWG back to the panel — and that wire has resistance. At 20mA sealed current, 50 feet of 18AWG (about 3.2 ohms for the pair) drops roughly 0.06V — negligible. But at 150mA inrush those same wires drop almost 0.5V and the coil terminal voltage sags to ~23.5V for the first 50ms. That's still fine. Now imagine a marginal transformer with a loose secondary lug, or a long run of 22AWG pulled years ago: the inrush sag can pull coil voltage down to 18-19V on a coil that needs 21V minimum to pull in. The relay chatters — it partially energizes, the inrush sags the voltage below pickup, it drops out, voltage recovers, it tries again. Every chatter cycle arcs the main contacts and burns the coil faster. The momentary button circuit exposes this problem on every press because there's no seal-in to ride through that first inrush dip.

Push button contacts have their own failure signature in a coil-only circuit like this one. Because the button is the only thing holding the coil, any intermittent on that contact kills the output immediately. A worn button with pitted contacts may pass the voltage when you probe it open-circuit — you'll read a solid 24V through it — but under load, when the coil is drawing current, the contact's oxide film or a pit creates a small resistance. That resistance drops voltage. You press, the coil chatters or fails to pull in fully, you assume the button is fine because the meter read good. That's the 'voltage-drop-under-load' trap. The correct test: with the coil connected and the button held, measure the voltage drop ACROSS the button contacts while current is flowing. A good contact reads less than 0.1V drop. Anything over 0.5V across a closed contact means the contact is failing, and you've proven it with the load attached where it matters.

When you release a momentary button on a coil circuit, the coil's magnetic field collapses — and that stored energy has to go somewhere. Without a suppression device, it fires back through the circuit as a voltage spike. In a simple 24VDC circuit with no suppression, a reasonably large relay coil can generate 100-200V spikes for microseconds. Those spikes see the now-open button contact gap as the easiest path, and they arc across it. Over thousands of press-release cycles, those arcs erode the contact faces, increasing contact resistance, eventually leading to the coil not fully energizing even when the button appears to work. The lesson here is foundational: the momentary-only circuit isn't just impractical for keeping a motor running — it's also mechanically harsh on the button because the coil's inductive kick has only one place to go.

failureModes.symptom: Machine runs fine while operator holds Start with both hands, but a quick press-and-release never starts it — coil fires then immediately drops
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage across the button contacts while holding it closed with load connected. A good contact reads under 0.1V drop. If it reads 2-5V drop under the coil's inrush current, the contacts are corroded or pitted — the voltage sag kills pickup. Open-circuit test of the button will read full 24V and fool you.

failureModes.symptom: Relay chatters and buzzes on every Start attempt — you can hear it trying to pull in, 10-20 times per second, never latching solid
failureModes.catch: Measure coil terminal voltage during the press attempt. If it collapses from 24V to 16-18V and bounces, the problem is wire-run voltage drop under inrush OR a weak power supply. Disconnect the coil and measure supply voltage under no-load vs full-load — a sag of more than 10% on your 24VDC supply means the transformer or PS is undersized or has a loose connection.

failureModes.symptom: Button has to be pressed at exactly the right speed — slow pushes work, fast jabs don't — suggesting contact bounce
failureModes.catch: Contact bounce on the input side: a mechanical push button closes and re-opens 2-10 times in the first 1-5 milliseconds. In a momentary-only coil circuit, if the coil inrush is slow to pull and the bounce opens the circuit before current stabilizes, the relay chatters or never holds. Confirm by measuring coil voltage on a storage oscilloscope — you'll see milisecond-width voltage notches on press.

failureModes.symptom: Button visibly presses and releases fine, but after 3-4 months of heavy use the machine takes multiple press attempts to start
failureModes.catch: Remove the button and measure contact resistance with your lowest ohm scale (not voltage). A new button reads under 0.05 ohms. A failing button reads 1-10 ohms. At 20mA sealed current, even 5 ohms across the contact only drops 0.1V — not enough to matter — but at 150mA inrush that same 5 ohms drops 0.75V, which can prevent pickup on a marginal supply. Worn contacts from repeated inrush arcing are the cause.

failureModes.symptom: Coil fries — relay hot to the touch within minutes, insulation smell — even though it's only being powered momentarily
failureModes.catch: No flyback suppression on a DC coil will kill the button fast but the coil itself is usually the victim if someone wired an AC coil into a DC circuit, or vice versa. Verify the coil rating stamped on the relay body matches the supply. Also check for a shorted flyback diode: a shorted diode puts a dead short on the DC supply every time the coil energizes, causing supply voltage to collapse and coil current to spike. Measure the diode out-of-circuit — it should block in one direction and pass in the other.

warStory: We had a conveyor on a bag-filling line — operator complained it only ran 'sometimes' when he pressed Start, and when it didn't catch he'd hold the button down for 2-3 seconds until it grabbed. I watched him run it for ten minutes, figured the button was fine since it clearly worked, and nearly wrote it off as operator technique. Then I put a meter on the coil terminals WHILE he held the button down and watched the voltage: it sagged to 17.5V on inrush and then climbed back to 23.8V sealed — the coil was just barely pulling in. Traced it to a corroded ring terminal on the 24VDC supply bus, maybe 4 ohms under load. That one bad crimp had been causing the chatter for six months and nobody connected the coil hum to the contact failure that was developing on the Start button from the repeated arcing.
fieldNotes: A 24VDC relay coil typically draws 6-10x more current (inrush) than its sealed holding current for the first 20-100ms — design your button contact ratings and wire sizing for that peak, not the steady-state value.

Voltage drop across a CLOSED contact should be under 0.1V DC under load. Anything over 0.5V means the contact is failing — an open-circuit voltage reading will never catch this; you must measure with load current flowing.

Always install a flyback diode (1N4007 or equivalent) across a DC relay coil, cathode toward positive supply. Without it, coil release spikes can reach 100-200V and arc-erode button contacts on every release cycle.

Pickup voltage for most relay coils is 75-85% of rated voltage; drop-out is 10-30% of rated. A 24VDC coil typically needs at least 18-20V to pull in — a voltage sag during inrush that dips below this threshold causes chatter even if steady-state voltage is fine.

quickCheck.symptom: You walk up to a machine where the relay is warm to the touch and there's a faint burnt-insulation smell, even though the Start button has only ever been pressed briefly, never held down. The coil is 24VDC. What do you check first?

options.label: Check the coil's rated voltage/type stamped on the relay body against the actual supply — confirm it's not a mismatched AC coil on a DC circuit or vice versa, and check the flyback diode for a short
options.why: Heat and a burning smell on a coil that's only briefly energized points to the coil itself being the victim, not a wiring or button problem. The two most likely causes per this lesson are a coil/supply mismatch (wrong voltage or AC/DC rating) or a shorted flyback diode, which dead-shorts the DC supply every energize cycle and spikes coil current. Checking the stamped rating and testing the diode out-of-circuit (block one way, pass the other) finds both fast.

options.label: Assume the button is being held too long by the operator and coach them on proper technique
options.why: A coil only heats up like this from sustained current draw it wasn't designed for, and the symptom described is heat even on brief presses — that rules out simple 'held too long' operator behavior. This guess also skips checking the actual hardware, which is where this lesson says the real fault (mismatched coil rating or a shorted diode) will be found.

options.label: Replace the Start button, since a hot relay usually means the button contacts are arcing
options.why: Arcing button contacts erode over time and eventually cause chatter or failure to pull in — they don't make the relay coil itself run hot on brief presses. Heat at the coil points to a problem with the coil circuit itself (wrong rating or shorted suppression diode), not the button, so swapping the button here treats the wrong component.

title: The Seal-In: Make the Relay Hold Itself On
objective: Use one of the relay's own NO contacts as a parallel seal-in path so it keeps its coil powered after the button is released.
plainExplanation: Here's the clever trick that fixes the release problem: wire one of the relay's OWN now-closed contacts in parallel with the Start button, feeding the coil. Press Start → coil energizes → that extra contact closes → and now it feeds the coil all by itself. Let go of Start and the coil stays powered through its own contact. The relay is 'sealed in' or 'latched.' This is the heartbeat of nearly every motor-control circuit in the plant. New problem, though: it's latched on with no way to turn it OFF.
interactiveElement: Learner drags a 'seal-in' wire from a spare NO contact to parallel the Start button, then taps Start and releases — the Running light stays on. A highlighted current-path animation shows electricity now flowing through the relay's own contact.
checkQuestion: After you release the Start button, what keeps the coil energized in a latched circuit?
checkAnswer: One of the relay's own NO contacts, wired in parallel with the Start button, has closed and now feeds the coil itself. The relay holds its own power — it's 'sealed in.' But there's still no way to stop it.
buildsOn: m11-momentary-button-problem (solve the release problem with the relay's own contact — original print 8)
deepDive: The seal-in contact has a current rating that most people ignore until something burns up. That auxiliary NO contact on a DPDT relay or motor starter is typically rated 10A at 120VAC or 6A at 240VAC — significantly lower than the main power contacts. What it carries is the coil holding current: a sealed 24VDC coil draws maybe 50–100mA once it's pulled in, trivial compared to the contact's capacity. But the coil inrush at the moment the armature first pulls is 6–10x the sealed current — a 24VDC coil that holds at 80mA may spike to 600mA for the first 15–50 milliseconds while the air gap closes and the magnetic circuit saturates. If your supply is marginal and that inrush causes a voltage sag that prevents full pull-in, the coil chatters at inrush current indefinitely, hammering the seal-in contact with repetitive arcs. A coil rated 80mA sealing current will burn out in hours running at 600mA inrush continuously. You hear it as a rapid 60Hz buzz from the panel — by the time it's audible the coil insulation is already cooking.

Voltage drop under load kills more seal-in circuits than anything else. You measure 24VDC at the power supply terminals with no load — clean, steady. But run a wire from that supply through 50 feet of undersized 22 AWG, through a Stop button with a loose terminal, through the Start button, and then to the coil — that coil sees maybe 18V at inrush because the current pulse drops voltage across every series resistance in the path. At 18V a 24VDC relay may or may not fully pull in, and if it does, the armature doesn't seat solidly — air gap remains, inrush current stays elevated, coil overheats. The fix is a meter on the coil terminals while you press Start, not at the supply. If coil voltage collapses more than 15% during inrush (below ~20V on a 24VDC circuit), you have a wiring or supply problem, not a coil problem. Industrial control specs typically allow ±15% coil voltage tolerance; below 85% of rated the relay won't pull in reliably. Above 110% the insulation degrades faster from heat. Matching the coil voltage label is necessary but not sufficient — you have to prove voltage at the coil under load.

The seal-in contact itself is the most mechanically stressed contact in the circuit because it cycles at inrush current — it opens and closes only when the coil is transitioning, so every time someone presses Start or Stop, that contact makes or breaks at coil inrush, not the small sealed current. Over thousands of cycles, the silver-alloy contact surface pits from the arc. A pitted seal-in contact doesn't weld shut the way a high-current power contact does — instead it develops a thin layer of silver oxide and arc deposits that raises contact resistance. You might see 0V across a good closed contact; a degraded seal-in can show 4–8V dropped across it while passing 100mA, which means the coil only sees 16V instead of 24V. That's enough to prevent clean pull-in or cause mid-run dropout. Test it: measure voltage across the CLOSED seal-in contact while the circuit is latched. Any reading above 0.5V means the contact is failing. Contacts don't fail all at once — they get flaky first, which is why the machine runs fine in the morning and ghosts out after lunch when the panel warms up and contact resistance rises further with temperature.

A sneak path through the seal-in circuit is a classic trap during troubleshooting. The seal-in contact is wired in parallel with the Start button, which means at the nodes A (one side of Start/seal-in) and B (the other side, feeding the coil through the Stop button), you have two paths between the same two nodes. If you have a ground fault on the coil or a leakage path to neutral anywhere in that parallel loop, you can end up with the coil energized through a path you didn't intend — and when you open the Stop button, power still reaches the coil through the fault and the machine won't stop. This is not hypothetical: condensation in a conduit, a chafed wire on a panel edge, a coil with moisture-degraded insulation — any of these creates a parallel path around your Stop button. The test: open the Stop button and the coil should immediately drop out. If it doesn't, you have a sneak path supplying the coil from somewhere else. Take nothing for granted in a wet plant or an aging panel.

failureModes.symptom: Machine starts but drops out the instant the Start button is released — every single time, operator reports 'Start button doesn't work'
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage at the seal-in contact terminals with the circuit latched (hold Start and watch transition). If the contact reads open (0V on each terminal but no coil voltage sustained after release) the seal-in contact is open-circuit — failed mechanically or its wiring is broken. Resistance across it with power off should be under 1 ohm when contact is manually pushed closed.

failureModes.symptom: Machine latches on but won't stop when the Stop button is pressed — operator has to trip the breaker to kill it
failureModes.catch: With the circuit 'latched on', open the Stop button physically (or disconnect it) and verify coil drops out. If it stays energized, measure voltage at the coil from both the Stop-side node and the neutral rail — a sneak path will show control voltage present from an unexpected direction. Check for insulation breakdown on the coil leads or a parallel wire fault.

failureModes.symptom: Intermittent latch — runs fine all morning then starts dropping out randomly mid-shift, especially on hot days or after a rain
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage ACROSS the closed seal-in contact while latched (not at the supply, not at the coil — across the contact itself). Good contact reads under 0.5V. A reading of 2–8V across a 'closed' contact fingerprints a high-resistance pitted or oxidized contact. Wiggling the seal-in wire at the terminal strip while monitoring coil voltage will often cause the dropout to reproduce if the root cause is a loose terminal rather than the contact face.

failureModes.symptom: Coil hums loudly, relay chatters, overheats — sometimes latches, sometimes doesn't, gets hot to the touch within minutes
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage directly at the coil terminals at the moment of Start press. Chatter is almost always coil voltage collapsing below pickup threshold (below 85% of rated). On 24VDC: reading below ~20V at coil terminals during inrush confirms supply or wiring voltage drop. Check supply current rating — a coil inrush of 600mA through a 500mA supply will sag the rail and prevent clean pull-in. Also verify AC hum on a DC coil is not half-wave rectification from a failing power supply (coil will chatter at 60 or 120 Hz).

failureModes.symptom: Relay latches and holds, but the seal-in contact arc-burns and pits out rapidly — has to be replaced every few months
failureModes.catch: This points to the coil being supplied from a source with high inrush — either an oversized or undersized coil, or more commonly, a slow-blowing supply with poor transient response that lets the inrush spike go high. Verify coil voltage is within 100–110% of rated. Install a suppression diode (for DC coils) or MOV/RC snubber across the coil — without suppression the inductive kick at dropout can be 10–20x the supply voltage, causing a heavy arc every time the seal-in contact opens at dropout.

warStory: We had a conveyor infeed motor at a corrugating plant — the operator swore the Start button was broken because the motor would run for exactly as long as he held it and then stop. I went out there expecting a stuck button, but the Start button tested fine. Pulled the control panel and found the seal-in auxiliary contact on the motor starter had a terminal screw backed out one turn — just enough to make contact on the wire under vibration while everything was warm, but it had finally worked itself loose overnight. The wire looked seated, the terminal looked tight until you put a screwdriver on it. Three minutes with a screwdriver, torqued to spec. The operator had been babysitting that motor by hand for two shifts because nobody thought to check the terminal on the seal-in contact — everyone went straight to the Start button because that's what the symptom pointed at.
fieldNotes: Seal-in contact rated voltage/current is on the relay datasheet as 'auxiliary contact rating' — it is always lower than the main contact rating; never use a main power contact spec to judge the auxiliary. Typical auxiliary: 10A at 120VAC, 6A at 240VAC.
A 24VDC coil draws roughly 50–100mA sealed, but 400–800mA inrush for the first 20–50ms — always size your supply for inrush, not sealed current, or the supply sags and the relay chatters.

Measure coil voltage at the coil terminals under load (during Start press), not at the supply — a 24VDC supply can read 24.1V at the panel and 17V at the coil if the control wiring has high resistance. 85% of rated voltage is the typical minimum pull-in threshold.

Always put a suppression diode (1N4007 or equivalent, banded end to positive) across a DC coil — without it, the inductive kickback spike at dropout can reach 200–400V on a 24VDC circuit and arc-pit your seal-in contact every cycle.

quickCheck.symptom: You've got a latched conveyor motor running. You press the Stop button — normally that opens the circuit and drops the coil out. Instead, the motor just keeps running like nothing happened. You swap in a brand-new Stop button and the problem is identical.

options.label: Suspect a sneak path: check for an unintended parallel route feeding the coil (moisture in a conduit, a chafed wire, leakage through the coil insulation) that's bypassing the Stop button entirely
options.why: Since a fresh Stop button didn't fix it, the button itself was never the problem — power is reaching the coil through some other path that lives outside the button. The module is explicit that a ground fault or leakage path anywhere in the seal-in's parallel loop can feed the coil even with Stop open, and the giveaway test is exactly what you just ran: open/replace Stop and the coil should immediately drop out — if it doesn't, look for a sneak path, not a bad button.

options.label: Conclude the seal-in auxiliary contact has welded shut, so the coil can never lose power no matter what you do to Start or Stop
options.why: A welded seal-in contact only explains the coil staying powered through that specific contact — but replacing the Stop button ruled out the button, not the seal-in contact, and a welded aux contact doesn't fully explain power still flowing when the coil circuit is opened elsewhere. This jumps past the actual test you already ran, which points at an alternate path, not a single stuck contact.

options.label: Assume the coil itself is shorted internally and will stay energized regardless of any control wiring
options.why: An internally shorted coil is a real failure mode elsewhere in electrical work, but it doesn't match this module's troubleshooting logic: the module ties 'won't stop when Stop is pressed' directly to a sneak path around the Stop button, tested by opening/replacing Stop and watching for the coil to drop. A shorted coil also wouldn't typically let the relay pull in and run normally otherwise — it's a much less likely explanation than a leakage path the module specifically calls out.

title: The Stop Button: The Classic 3-Wire Start/Stop
objective: Insert a Normally Closed Stop button ahead of Start to complete the canonical 3-wire seal-in control circuit.
plainExplanation: To turn the latched relay off, we add a NORMALLY CLOSED Stop button BEFORE the Start button in the line. Because it's NC, it normally lets power through, so the latch works fine. Press Stop and it opens for an instant, breaking the feed to the coil — the relay drops out, the seal-in contact opens, and everything resets. This is THE circuit: press Start to run-and-latch, press Stop to break-and-reset. It's called the 3-wire control or seal-in circuit, and you'll see it on machines everywhere.
interactiveElement: The full latch with a NC Stop button added in series ahead of Start. Learner runs the sequence: Start (latches on), release (stays on), Stop (drops out). A wiring callout labels '3-wire control' and shows why Stop must be NC.
checkQuestion: Why must the Stop button be Normally CLOSED, and where does it go in the circuit?
checkAnswer: It's wired in series ahead of the Start button. It must be NC so it normally passes power and lets the latch hold; pressing it opens the path for a moment, dropping out the coil and resetting the circuit. That's the classic 3-wire start/stop.
buildsOn: m12-seal-in-latch (add the missing OFF — the canonical latch is now complete, original print 9)
deepDive: The 3-wire circuit's genius is also its fragility: every component in series with the coil is a potential single-point failure. The Stop button sits at line voltage — typically 120VAC in legacy plants or 24VDC in modern ones — in series with the Start button and the seal-in contact. Current through that path is small, usually under 100mA on a 24VDC circuit, because you're only driving a coil. But 'small current' doesn't mean 'harmless terminal.' A loose screw on the Stop button's NC terminal creates resistance. At 24VDC and 50mA, even 20 ohms of bad connection drops a volt — harmless. But if that same terminal gets hot from vibration cycling and oxidizes to 200 ohms, you drop 10V at the terminal and the coil sees only 14V instead of 24V. Coil rated 24VDC will typically hold in down to roughly 85% of rated voltage (about 20.4V) but that margin evaporates fast, and you get intermittent nuisance trips that disappear the moment you wiggle the wires.

Here's the failure nobody expects: the Stop button's NC contacts pit and carbon-track over years of normal operation. Each time an operator presses Stop, they're opening that contact while the coil is still energized — the coil's collapsing magnetic field tries to sustain current and creates a voltage spike across the opening contacts. Without an arc-suppression snubber or MOV across the coil, that spike can be 5-10x the supply voltage for microseconds. At 24VDC that's up to 240V transient across a 24V-rated switch contact. Over thousands of cycles, you get carbon deposits between the NC contact faces. The contact now passes leakage current even when 'open' — and in a 24VDC circuit with a sensitive modern relay, that leakage can be enough to hold the coil partially energized, causing slow dropout or chattering. You press Stop, the machine hesitates for a half-second, and then drops. That half-second is the carbon bridge bleeding just enough current to fight the spring.

The 'sneak path' version of this fault is more dangerous. Suppose you have two Stop buttons wired in a panel — one at the machine, one at a remote station — both NC, both in series. An electrician replaces the remote Stop button and accidentally installs a NO button instead of NC. The circuit appears to work fine: press local Stop, machine drops. But press remote Stop and it does nothing, because the button was already open at rest, breaking the series path anyway. Now you have a dead E-stop station with no indication. The reverse is equally bad: if someone bridges across the Stop button terminals for 'temporary' testing and forgets to remove the jumper, the Stop button is permanently bypassed. Press Stop all day, the coil stays sealed in, the machine won't stop. This is why on safety-rated circuits you never jumper Stop buttons and why 3-wire control in NFPA 79 / IEC 60204 applications requires the Stop function be normally-closed, hardwired, and monitored — exactly because a wiring mistake to this one component defeats the entire stop mechanism.

The inrush vs sealed-current distinction matters for the Stop button's contact rating. When the relay coil first energizes, it draws inrush current — typically 6-10x the sealed (holding) current — for the first 20-50 milliseconds until the armature seats and the magnetic circuit is complete. A 24VDC coil rated at 50mA sealed might draw 300-500mA for that first 50ms. The Start button contacts and the Stop button contacts carry this inrush every time someone presses Start. A 3A-rated 24VDC contact can handle this all day. But if someone installs a cheap 0.5A logic-level switch as the Start button to save money, those contacts are being hit with 500mA inrush on every start — and they will pit and burn in months. The Stop button only sees inrush in one edge case: if it's pressed and immediately released before the relay fully seats. In normal operation the Stop button breaks the sealed current, which is low, and that's why NC Stop buttons outlast NO Start buttons by a wide margin in high-cycle applications.

failureModes.symptom: Machine stops randomly during the shift, restarts normally when operator presses Start again, maintenance can't reproduce during investigation
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage across the Stop button NC contacts (not through the circuit — across the contact itself) while the machine runs. A good contact reads 0V when closed. A loose or corroded NC terminal reads 1-5V across itself as resistance develops; voltage rises as the terminal heats.
