Thermally intermittent — reproduces after the panel has been running for an hour, goes away when you open the door and it cools down.

failureModes.symptom: Machine won't stop when operator presses Stop button at the remote pendant; local panel Stop button works fine
failureModes.catch: With machine running, measure voltage at the remote Stop button terminals: if you read full supply voltage (24VDC or 120VAC) across both terminals of an 'NC' stop button that was recently replaced, the replacement is a NO button wired in. The circuit sees it as already-open, so pressing it does nothing — it was open at rest. Visually confirm button type or measure continuity unpowered: NC reads 0 ohms at rest, NO reads open.

failureModes.symptom: Machine won't stop at all; pressing Stop button has no effect; machine continues running
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage at the Stop button terminals with the machine running. If you read 0V across the button (correct for a closed NC contact) but the machine still won't stop, look for a jumper or bridge across the Stop terminals — a wire someone left in 'temporarily.' If you read 0V and no jumper exists, check the Stop button wiring: both terminals may be landed on the same bus bar, effectively jumpering the button out of circuit.

failureModes.symptom: Machine hesitates 0.5-1 second after Stop is pressed before actually dropping out; gets worse over time
failureModes.catch: Disconnect power. Measure resistance across the Stop button NC contacts when NOT pressed (should read under 1 ohm on a good contact). A reading of 5-50 ohms indicates carbon tracking from arc erosion. Confirm by inspecting contact faces under a light — you'll see carbon scoring. Also check for missing arc-suppression MOV or snubber across the coil; its absence accelerates this failure. Replace the button and install a coil suppressor.

failureModes.symptom: New installation: Start latches, but pressing Stop causes the machine to immediately restart without anyone pressing Start again
failureModes.catch: This is a wiring error — Stop and Start buttons are swapped. The NC Stop button is wired where the NO Start button should be, and vice versa. Pressing 'Stop' (actually the NO Start contact) closes a path to the coil. The seal-in then holds it. Verify by measuring: the button labeled Stop should read closed (0 ohms) at rest; if it reads open at rest, it is a NO contact installed in the Stop position. Swap the buttons.

warStory: Called out to a packaging line at 2 AM because the line wouldn't stop when product jammed — operator was pressing the pendant Stop button and the conveyor kept running, jamming product into the jam and making it worse. I pulled the pendant apart expecting a failed contact, but the NC Stop button measured perfect. Traced back to the panel and found a 6-inch jumper wire across the Stop button terminals in the main disconnect enclosure — a day-shift electrician had put it in three weeks earlier to 'temporarily' bypass the button while he chased a nuisance-trip gremlin, then got called to another emergency and never pulled it. The jumper had been there through six shifts, nobody noticed because the local Stop button on the panel door still worked (it was upstream in the same series string), and the pendant Stop was just silently bypassed. Pulled the jumper, Stop button worked instantly, and I wrote up the jumper as a near-miss safety event — because on a machine with a rotating die, a bypassed stop station is a finger-removal event waiting for its moment.
fieldNotes: NC Stop button in a 3-wire circuit must be wired IN SERIES with the coil supply path — never in parallel. A Stop button wired in parallel would add a path instead of breaking one, and pressing it would have zero effect or could even cause unsafe behavior.

A relay coil's sealed (holding) current is typically 1/6 to 1/10 of its inrush current. For a 24VDC coil drawing 50mA sealed, expect 300-500mA inrush for the first 20-50ms on every Start press — size Start button contacts accordingly (minimum 3A rated).

NFPA 79 (electrical standard for industrial machinery) requires that the Stop function use normally-closed contacts and that loss of power to the control circuit results in a safe (stopped) state — the 3-wire NC Stop design satisfies this requirement by design: cut power anywhere in the control circuit and the coil drops.

Always install a coil suppression device (MOV or RC snubber, matched to coil voltage) across relay and contactor coils. Without it, contact-opening transients on DC circuits can reach 10x supply voltage and will carbon-track your Stop button contacts within 50,000 cycles.

quickCheck.symptom: You walk up to a machine mid-shift. The operator says it's been dropping out randomly every hour or so, always restarts fine when they hit Start again, but nobody can catch it in the act. The panel door has been open for the last 20 minutes while you've been standing there and it hasn't happened once.

options.label: Close the panel door, let it run undisturbed, and check voltage across the NC Stop button's own contact (not through the circuit) after it's been running warm for a while
options.why: This fault only shows up after the panel heats up over time and disappears the moment you open the door and let it cool — classic thermally-intermittent loose/corroded terminal. Reading voltage across the closed contact itself (should be ~0V) while it's hot lets you catch the resistance building up in real time instead of chasing a fault that vanishes as soon as you start investigating.

options.label: Replace the Stop button immediately since intermittent drops are always a worn-out NC contact
options.why: Jumping to a parts swap skips the diagnostic step and risks missing the real cause — a worn contact is one possibility, but a loose/corroded terminal, a marginal coil voltage, or wiring resistance elsewhere in the series string can look identical. Replacing parts without confirming with a meter reading is guessing, not troubleshooting.

options.label: Conclude it's a Normally Open button installed by mistake, since that would explain the machine dropping out on its own
options.why: A NO button wired into an NC Stop position fails every single time — the coil could never latch in the first place, so the machine wouldn't run for an hour between drops. This fault doesn't match a miswired button; the hour-long run time before each drop points to something that develops over time, like heat-driven resistance at a terminal.

title: Swap the Bulb for a Motor Starter (Contactor)
objective: Replace the load with a motor starter/contactor and understand it as a heavy-duty relay that handles big amperage and voltage.
plainExplanation: Our circuit can control a light bulb, but plants run motors that draw far more current than a little control relay can handle. So we replace the bulb with a MOTOR STARTER (contactor) — which raises the question: 'isn't that just a relay?' Yes! It's a beefy relay with big contacts rated for high amperage and voltage. The neat part: our same little 24VDC Start/Stop circuit energizes the contactor's coil, and the contactor does the heavy lifting. Small safe control circuit, big dangerous power circuit, cleanly separated.
interactiveElement: Drag-and-drop swap: pull the bulb out of the latched circuit and drop in a contactor symbol. The 24VDC control side stays identical; a 'power side' panel appears showing the contactor's heavy main contacts waiting for big power.
checkQuestion: What is a motor starter / contactor, really, and why use one instead of switching the motor with our control relay directly?
checkAnswer: It's just a heavy-duty relay with big contacts rated for high amperage and voltage. We use it because our small 24VDC control relay can't carry a motor's current — the little circuit energizes the contactor's coil, and the contactor switches the big power safely.
buildsOn: m13-stop-button-3wire (keep the exact same latch, just swap the load to something industrial — original print 10)
deepDive: A pilot light in our control circuit might draw 5–15 milliamps at 24VDC — that little general-purpose relay handles it without breaking a sweat. Now swap in a 5-HP motor. At 480V 3-phase that motor draws roughly 7.6 full-load amps per leg, and at startup the inrush can hit 6–8x that — 45–60 amps for several seconds. Your little control relay is probably rated 10A resistive, maybe 3A inductive. Put 480V line voltage through it and you've exceeded both its voltage insulation rating and its current capacity. The contacts aren't built for that arc energy. They'll weld shut on the first close, or they'll get pitted so badly they won't make solid contact on the second try. The rule is non-negotiable: the contact rating must exceed the load's voltage AND its inductive-inrush current, with margin.

A contactor is that same relay idea scaled up for the job. The main contacts are physically large — mass-produced NEMA Size 1 contactors carry 27A continuous, Size 2 carries 45A. The contact material is silver-cadmium oxide or silver-tin oxide, chosen because it resists welding and arc erosion under inductive loads. The contact gap on a contactor main pole is much wider than a control relay to interrupt high-voltage arcs cleanly; some have arc chutes or horns to stretch and cool the arc on opening. The coil is still a standard electromagnetic coil — often 120VAC or 24VDC — and that's the critical design principle: the coil voltage is completely separate from the main contact voltage. Your 24VDC Start/Stop circuit energizes a 24VDC coil on a contactor whose main contacts are switching 480VAC at 30 amps. Control side never sees the power side. That separation is how a plant electrician can safely troubleshoot the control circuit with a meter while the power side is locked out on the other side of the enclosure.

Coil inrush is a real factor even on the contactor's own coil. When you first apply voltage to any electromagnetic coil, the initial inrush current can be 6–10x the sealed (holding) current — for a 120VAC contactor coil this might mean a 600mA inrush collapsing to 60mA once the armature seats and back-EMF builds. This matters for two reasons. First, whatever's switching the contactor coil — usually your control relay — has to handle that inrush. A 10A-rated control relay contact switching a contactor coil sees nowhere near its rating, so no problem. But if you paralleled multiple contactors on one control relay output, the stacked inrush might matter. Second, if supply voltage drops during that inrush period and the armature can't fully seat, the coil stays in the high-inrush zone, drawing 6–10x sealed current continuously. It gets hot fast. A coil that chatters — seating and releasing rapidly because voltage is marginal — will burn up in minutes. This is the same root failure whether it's a $4 control relay or a $200 contactor: low control voltage causes chatter, chatter causes heat, heat burns the coil.

The motor starter is a contactor plus an overload relay — and the overload is what makes it a 'starter' rather than just a contactor. The overload relay monitors current on the motor legs and trips if the motor exceeds its nameplate current for long enough. This protects the motor windings from the slow thermal death of running overloaded — where the motor isn't drawing enough current to blow a fuse or trip a breaker, but it's running 15–20% over nameplate and the winding insulation is degrading at 10°C per 10% overtemp above rating. When the overload trips, it opens a normally-closed auxiliary contact in the control circuit — breaking the coil circuit just like a Stop button. This is why you check the overload first when a motor trips on an otherwise-healthy circuit: find the reset button, read the trip indicator, reset it, and monitor amperage on restart. Don't just reset and walk away.

failureModes.symptom: Contactor closes on Start but immediately re-opens; motor runs for under a second then drops out
failureModes.catch: Check the overload relay — thermal overload tripped, NC auxiliary contact open. Clip an ammeter on L1 to the motor; if running amps exceed nameplate FLA by more than 10-15%, you have a mechanical or electrical motor problem, not a controls problem. Reset overload ONLY after finding the cause.

failureModes.symptom: Contactor coil hums loudly or chatters continuously; may burn up within minutes if not caught
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage AT the coil terminals while the Start button is held — not at the supply, AT the coil. A coil rated 120VAC needs at least 104V (87% of rating) to hold in cleanly. If you read 120V at the panel and 85V at the coil under load, you have a wiring or connection resistance problem. Every volt drop means a volt less of closing force.

failureModes.symptom: Motor won't start; pressing Start produces no audible contactor click and no movement
failureModes.catch: Measure for control voltage at the contactor coil terminals. If 24VDC (or 120VAC, whatever the coil rating) is present at the coil terminals and it still won't pull in, the coil is open — ohmmeter across the coil terminals with power OFF should read the coil resistance (typically 50–2000 ohms depending on voltage and size). An open coil reads OL. Zero ohms means shorted — replace the contactor.

failureModes.symptom: Motor starts but doesn't stop when Stop is pressed; or contactor stays closed after power loss
failureModes.catch: Welded main contacts. With power locked out, try to manually depress and release the contactor manually (some have a test button). If the main contacts won't separate, or if a clamp meter shows current still flowing on the motor leg with the coil de-energized, the contacts are welded — almost always caused by repeated operation under a severe overload or a starting fault that caused high arc energy.

failureModes.symptom: Control circuit works perfectly — coil energizes — but motor doesn't run or runs weak on one phase
failureModes.catch: Power-side fault, not control-side. With the contactor closed, clamp-meter each motor leg at the T1/T2/T3 terminals. A missing or low phase shows up immediately. Could be a blown fuse on one leg of the disconnect feeding the contactor, or a burned contactor main contact on that leg. Single-phasing a 3-phase motor causes it to run hot and draw high current on the remaining two legs — the overload should trip, but if it's not set correctly, the motor will cook.

warStory: We had a packaging line that would run fine for two hours then trip the overload every time, same machine, same shift, no jams. Maintenance kept resetting the overload and walking away — called it a nuisance trip. I clamped all three legs on the motor while it was running and found L2 reading 23A while L1 and L3 were at 14A — lopsided as hell. Pulled the contactor and found the L2 main contact burned nearly flat; it was making resistance instead of a connection, dropping voltage to the motor on that leg and forcing the other two phases to compensate. The contactor had been "working" for three years in that degraded state, slowly getting worse, until one summer the motor room got hot enough to push it over the edge. The overload was doing its job exactly right — maintenance just wasn't asking why.
fieldNotes: NEMA Size 1 contactor: 27A continuous, typical for motors up to 7.5HP at 460V. Size 2: 45A, up to 15HP. Always match to motor FLA with margin, not just horsepower — nameplate wins.

Motor inrush (locked-rotor) current is typically 600–800% of full-load amps. A motor drawing 10A running may pull 60–80A for 2–8 seconds on start. Contactor main contacts and upstream fuses must handle this without nuisance-opening.

Overload relay trip class: Class 10 trips within 10 seconds at 600% FLA (for quick-to-stall loads), Class 20 within 20 seconds (standard motors), Class 30 within 30 seconds (high-inertia loads like fans). Wrong class = nuisance trips or no protection.

The overload relay's heater element or current setting must be set to the motor's nameplate Full Load Amps — not the breaker size, not the wire size, not 'about right.' Oversized overloads are invisible insurance that never pays out.

quickCheck.symptom: You walk up to a motor that just tripped again. You press the reset button on the overload and the motor runs fine for a minute, then trips again. No jams, nothing visibly wrong. What do you check first before resetting it again?

options.label: Clamp-meter all three motor legs while it's running and compare readings to nameplate FLA and to each other
options.why: This is the right move: an unbalanced or over-FLA reading (like L2 running high while L1/L3 run normal) tells you whether this is a real overcurrent condition from a degraded contact, single-phasing, or a mechanical problem — instead of just resetting blind and letting the root cause keep degrading.

options.label: Bump the overload's trip class or current setting so it stops tripping so often
options.why: Changing the overload setting hides a real problem instead of finding it. The overload is protecting the motor windings from a genuine overcurrent condition — raising the setting just lets that condition run longer before anyone notices, exactly like the war-story contactor that degraded for years before finally cooking.

options.label: Assume it's a nuisance trip and keep resetting it each time it happens
options.why: Repeated trips with no jam and no obvious cause are the classic sign of a real underlying fault, not a nuisance — as in the war story, a burned contact caused three years of intermittent overload trips that everyone dismissed as nuisance trips until it got bad enough to matter.

title: The Overload Relay: the Motor's Bodyguard
objective: Know what a thermal overload relay is and what it protects (the motor, not the wires), how the heater/bimetal mechanism works, how to set the FLA dial off the motor nameplate, and why a trip opens the little 95-96 contact in the COIL circuit — never the motor current itself.
plainExplanation: Last lesson we said a motor starter is a contactor PLUS an overload relay. This is the overload's lesson — the RW27 that rides under our CWB contactor. Here's the split that confuses people: the fuses and breaker protect the WIRES from short circuits; the overload protects the MOTOR from itself. A motor that's overloaded doesn't blow fuses — it just quietly pulls 15 or 25% more current than its nameplate says, hour after hour, cooking its winding insulation. The overload catches exactly that. The motor's current flows through the overload's HEATER elements; the heat bends a BIMETAL strip (two metals that expand differently), and when the motor has been pulling too much for too long, the strip pushes a latch over and it TRIPS. And here's the punchline every new tech gets wrong: the overload does NOT break the motor current. When it trips, a little pilot-duty contact — terminals 95 and 96, normally closed, wired in series with the contactor's coil — pops open. Same effect as somebody pressing Stop: the coil drops, and the CONTACTOR's big contacts stop the motor. The overload is the brain; the contactor is still the muscle.
interactiveElement: The Trip Lab: press START, then drag the motor-load slider past the RW27's FLA dial and watch the heater elements glow while the bimetal heat meter climbs to TRIP — 95-96 pops open, the coil drops, the contactor stops the motor, and the amber trip light (97-98) comes on. The live meter strip runs on the real solver: hold START after a trip and watch the full 24V land ACROSS the open 95-96 — the hopscotch tell. Let the bimetal cool, press RESET, and notice the 3-wire circuit waits for a human. Then flip reset to AUTO and control to 2-WIRE and watch the motor restart by itself — the hazard, demonstrated. Drop phase L2 to see single-phasing race the trip. The Schematic tab runs the same circuit on the live engine for probing, and the 3D and cut-sheet tabs are the real RW27.
checkQuestion: When a thermal overload trips, what current does it actually interrupt — and what finally stops the motor?
checkAnswer: Almost none: the trip opens the 95-96 normally-closed contact wired in series with the contactor coil — pilot-duty current, milliamps to an amp. The coil drops out and the CONTACTOR's main contacts break the motor current. The overload's heaters carry and measure the motor current, but the overload never switches it.
buildsOn: m14-swap-bulb-for-motor-starter (starter = contactor + overload; this is the overload half, with the RW27 cut sheet in hand)
deepDive: Why a fuse can't do this job: branch-circuit protection has to survive the motor's start. A motor pulls several times its full-load amps for seconds every time it starts, so the fuses or breaker feeding it are sized way above nameplate current — which means a motor grinding along at 115-125% of nameplate never touches them. But that slow overcurrent is precisely what kills motors: winding insulation ages fast for every degree over its rating, and an overloaded motor runs hot all shift with nothing complaining. The overload relay is built as a thermal MODEL of the motor. Our RW27-2D3-D063 is a thermal, bi-metallic overload: the same current that warms the motor's windings warms the overload's heater elements, so the bimetal 'remembers' heat the same way the motor does. Fuse = the wire's bodyguard, sized for faults. Overload = the motor's bodyguard, sized for the slow kill.

The mechanism and the dial. The motor current — all three legs — passes through three heater elements (the RW27 direct-mounts onto CWB contactors, its three lugs plugging straight into the contactor's load side, T1/T2/T3). Each heater warms a bimetal strip: two dissimilar metals bonded together that bow as they heat because they expand at different rates. Enough bow, and the strip shoves the trip latch over — mechanical, no electronics, no supply voltage needed. The FLA dial (4.0 to 6.3 amps on this model) scales how much current makes 'enough' heat: you set it to the motor's NAMEPLATE full-load amps, and the device's trip curve takes it from there — holding at normal current, ultimately tripping around 120% and up. Set the dial high 'so it stops tripping' and you've simply fired the bodyguard. Then there's TRIP CLASS, which is a time rating at 6x the dial current: Class 10 trips within about 10 seconds at 6x, Class 20 within 20, Class 30 within 30. The class exists because starting current looks exactly like a fault to a thermal device — you pick the lowest class whose window your motor's start fits inside. Quick-starting pumps and compressors get Class 10 (which the RW27-2D3-D063 is); a loaded conveyor or big fan that takes 15 seconds to come up to speed needs 20 or 30, or its own start trips it every morning.

The contacts are the punchline. On a trip, the overload opens 95-96 — a small normally-closed, pilot-duty contact whose only job is to sit in series with the contactor coil, on the coil's return side of the rung. Open 95-96 and the coil drops exactly as if someone pressed Stop; the contactor's main poles do the actual motor-stopping. The RW27 also carries one normally-open aux, 97-98 (the cut sheet lists 1 N.O. plus 1 N.C.), which CLOSES on the same trip — that's your amber TRIP light or PLC alarm input, and it's the difference between a ten-second diagnosis and a mystery dead-motor call. Reset on this model is local — a button on the device — and reset MODE is a safety decision, not a convenience one. Manual reset means a human walks to the panel, which means a human looked at the machine. Automatic reset re-closes 95-96 by itself once the bimetal cools; combine that with 2-wire control (a maintained switch or float/thermostat that's still closed) and the motor restarts unattended — with somebody's hands in the jam they were clearing. Auto reset belongs on unattended equipment with 2-wire control ONLY where a surprise restart can't reach anyone, and plants write that rule in blood, not ink.

Two field skills finish this lesson. First, single-phasing: lose one leg of a running 3-phase motor (a blown fuse upstream, a burned lug) and the motor doesn't stop — it keeps turning on two legs at roughly 1.7x current while it overheats. The RW27 is phase-loss sensitive: its differential mechanism reacts to one cold heater beside two hot ones and trips faster than the current rise alone would justify — exactly the response single-phasing needs. Second, metering a tripped one. Dead motor, trip light on (or no light wired — worse): hold Start and hopscotch the coil string. You'll find 24V all the way through the Stop button, the Start button, and even THROUGH the de-energized coil — a coil passing no current drops no voltage — right up to terminal 95. Terminal 96 reads 0V. Full supply ACROSS 95-96 is the signature of the open: the overload is your problem. And if it won't reset, it isn't broken — the bimetal is still hot. It physically can't re-latch until it cools, which on a real device takes minutes. Walking away for a coffee is a legitimate troubleshooting step.

failureModes.symptom: Motor dead, no click, no hum — and the amber OL TRIP light is lit
failureModes.catch: Tripped overload: 95-96 is open in the coil string. Let it cool, reset, restart — and STAY. Clamp all three legs against nameplate FLA, because the trip is a symptom, not the disease.

failureModes.symptom: Overload trips every day on an otherwise healthy motor
failureModes.catch: Check the dial against the motor nameplate FLA before anything else — a dial set below nameplate nuisance-trips on legitimate load. Then clamp the three legs for imbalance: one leg high or dead means a supply or connection problem, not an overload problem.

failureModes.symptom: Motor hums but won't turn, and the overload trips within seconds
failureModes.catch: Locked rotor — the motor is pulling 6x-plus current and the Class 10 clock runs out fast. Look for the jammed load or seized bearing. The overload did its job; don't 'fix' it by cranking the dial.

failureModes.symptom: Tripped overload refuses to reset right after the trip
failureModes.catch: The bimetal is still hot and mechanically can't re-latch — that's design, not damage. Give it minutes to cool. Never force the button, and NEVER jumper 95 to 96 to get production running: that wire fires the motor's only bodyguard.

fieldNotes: Set the FLA dial to the motor NAMEPLATE amps — the overload's own trip curve already includes the allowed margin above that. 'Crank it up so it stops tripping' converts a protected motor into an unprotected one.

Trip class is a time budget at 6x current: Class 10 protects fastest, 20 and 30 exist for loads whose normal start would trip a 10. Pick the lowest class the start rides through — the RW27-2D3-D063 is a Class 10 device.
A tripped overload and a blown control fuse feel identical from the buttons: dead station. The 97-98 trip light is what tells them apart from across the room — wire one on every starter.
Full control voltage ACROSS 95-96 (with Start held) = tripped overload. 0V across it = it's closed and your open is elsewhere. Same hopscotch rule as everything else in the string.
If it won't reset, it's still hot. Cooling takes minutes on a real bimetal. The Trip Lab compresses that to seconds so you can practice the cycle — don't expect a 6-second reset on the floor.
warStory: A blower on a packing line started dropping its overload most afternoons. Production was losing patience, so somebody 'solved' it over a weekend with a two-inch jumper wire across 95 and 96 — just till Monday, they said. Tuesday morning the motor was dead for real: windings cooked black, and the room smelled like it. The sad part is what the overload had been trying to say. Amp readings going back three weeks — once we finally took them — showed running current creeping from 5.8 toward 7 amps on a 6.3 dial as a bearing ran dry. A ten-dollar grease job and a bearing swap would have ended the trips. Instead the plant bought a motor, a rewind rush charge, and a day of downtime. The overload was never the problem. It was the messenger, and they shot it.

quickCheck.symptom: A conveyor's overload has tripped twice in twenty minutes. The operator resets it, it runs a couple of minutes, trips again. He wants you to crank the FLA dial to maximum so the shift can finish.

options.label: Crank the dial to 6.3 A — production is waiting and the resets prove the motor still runs
options.why: The dial is set to nameplate FLA, period. Raising it doesn't remove the overcurrent — it just moves the damage into the motor windings and makes the MOTOR the thing that trips next, permanently. Repeated fast re-trips mean the current is genuinely high right now.

options.label: Clamp all three legs against the nameplate FLA while it runs — fast repeat trips mean real overcurrent (binding load, lost phase, low voltage), and the dial only gets touched if it doesn't match the nameplate
options.why: Two honest trips in twenty minutes is the overload doing its job on a real problem. The clamp meter settles it in one minute: all legs high = mechanical load or low voltage; one leg dead and two high = single-phasing; current actually at nameplate with the dial set low = the one case where the dial was the problem.

options.label: Replace the overload relay — tripping twice in one shift proves it's worn out
options.why: Thermal overloads rarely 'wear into' tripping — and a new one will trip on the same overcurrent for the same reason. Verify the current before condemning the messenger; swapping parts without a reading is the exact habit the troubleshooting method exists to break.

title: Using the Poles: 1 to Latch, 3 for 3-Phase
objective: Explain a 3-phase motor and how a 4PST starter uses one pole for the seal-in and three for the motor legs.
plainExplanation: Industrial motors usually run on THREE-PHASE power — three hot legs (L1, L2, L3) whose pressures take turns, which spins motors smoothly and powerfully. A motor starter is often a Quadruple Pole Single Throw (4PST) relay: four switches that all close together. The classic trick: use ONE of those four poles as the seal-in contact to latch our control circuit, and let the other THREE poles each carry one leg of the 3-phase power to the motor. One coil, four poles, two jobs done at once.
interactiveElement: A 4PST contactor with four poles drawn out; learner assigns pole 1 to the seal-in (it wires back to the coil) and poles 2-4 to L1/L2/L3 of a motor. Energize the coil and all four close together, latching control AND powering the motor.
checkQuestion: On a 4-pole motor starter, what are the four poles typically doing in the example?
checkAnswer: One pole is used as the seal-in contact to latch the control circuit on; the other three carry the three legs (L1, L2, L3) of 3-phase power to the motor. All four close together when the coil energizes.
buildsOn: m14-swap-bulb-for-motor-starter (now USE the starter's extra poles — original print 11)
deepDive: A 3-phase contactor isn't just three single-pole switches that happen to share a frame — the mechanical linkage that ties all four poles to one armature is doing critical safety work. All three motor legs must open and close within a few milliseconds of each other. If one pole's contact bridge lags because of a weak spring, worn pivot, or arc-product buildup on the guide rail, you get an asymmetric make or break: one leg hits 480V an instant before the others, and the motor sees a lopsided voltage pulse. On a start, that's a shudder and an overcurrent spike. On a stop, you're breaking current on one leg while the other two still feed the load — the lagging contact gets the full arc duty meant to be shared across three gaps. That one contact runs hotter, pits faster, and eventually welds.

Contact ratings on 3-phase contactors are stamped with two numbers you must both read: a voltage rating and a current rating, but there's a hidden third parameter — the utilization category. A contactor rated 'AC-3' at 32A is sized for squirrel-cage motor switching: high inrush on make (typically 6–10x full-load amps for a few hundred milliseconds) and breaking running current. An 'AC-1' rating on the same body handles resistive loads at full current both ways but isn't designed for inrush. Wire a 30HP motor to an AC-1 contactor and you're slamming 600+ amps through contacts rated for 32A steady — they arc-weld shut the first start, or erode so fast the contact gap disappears in weeks. The fourth pole used for the seal-in circuit is working in a completely different environment: switching a 24VDC coil circuit at milliamps. That's actually harder on contacts in a different way — DC arcs don't have a natural zero-crossing to self-extinguish, so even tiny DC arcs sustain themselves and slowly erode a contact face that would last years on AC. A relay manufacturer sized for that application; a spare contact bridge from the power poles will not give you the same service life.

Phase loss is the failure mode this 4-pole topology exists to prevent — and to make obvious why it matters, here's the physics. A 3-phase motor running on two legs doesn't just run slower; it tries to develop full torque with only 58% of its normal rotating field. It draws roughly 200–250% of rated full-load amps on the two remaining legs while the rotor heats at roughly 3–4x its normal rate. The overload relay watching those two surviving legs will eventually trip — but 'eventually' can be a minute or two on a lightly loaded motor, long enough to cook the winding insulation to a brown crisp. The failure pattern you find: one set of motor winding coils burned black, the others relatively clean. That asymmetry tells you it was phase loss, not a general overvoltage or locked-rotor event. The one contactor pole that dropped open — either welded contacts that looked closed but had no real contact area, or a broken contact bridge — is what killed a $4,000 motor.

The seal-in pole assignment sounds like a minor detail but it has a specific wiring consequence. The auxiliary contact used for the seal-in is NOT interchangeable with the power poles — in most contactors it is a physically separate, smaller contact block mounted on the side of the main body, rated maybe 10A at 300V and built for control-circuit service (AC-15 or DC-13 category). Some plants wire the seal-in off a surplus power pole using a spare terminal, which works until someone replaces the contactor with a physically different model that doesn't have a fourth power pole, or until the power-pole contact rating causes problems. The right practice: always wire the holding circuit through the dedicated auxiliary contact, keep all four power poles for the motor. If the contactor you're looking at has no auxiliary contact block and only three main poles, you need an add-on auxiliary contact block or a separate interposing relay for the seal-in — do not improvise with the power poles.

failureModes.symptom: Motor hums and draws high current on start, trips overload within seconds, smells hot — happens on every start attempt
failureModes.catch: Clamp meter on each leg at the contactor load terminals: one leg reads 0A (open or welded-open contact with no real conducting area). Verify with a contact resistance test across each power pole with the contactor manually held in — a good contact is under 1 milliohm; a pitted or welded-open contact reads 5–100 ohms or open-loop.

failureModes.symptom: Motor runs but runs hot and draws unequal current; one leg shows 140% of nameplate amps, the others look normal
failureModes.catch: Measure L-L voltage at the motor terminals under load with the motor running. Healthy 3-phase: all three L-L readings within 1% of each other. A loose power-pole termination or a partially pitted contact collapses one leg's voltage — you'll see, say, 478V / 476V / 431V — that 10% voltage unbalance causes a 50%+ current unbalance per NEMA MG1 rules.

failureModes.symptom: Contactor energizes (audible click, coil draws current) but motor doesn't start — running lights are on, overload has not tripped
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage at the motor terminal box on all three legs with the contactor pulled in. If one or more legs read 0V while the contactor coil is energized, a power contact bridge is mechanically closed but not making electrical contact — a welded-open bridge or broken carrier. This is distinct from the coil fault and requires the contactor body replacement, not a coil swap.

failureModes.symptom: Machine starts and runs, but releases itself (drops out) unpredictably after a few seconds to minutes — no faults, no alarms, no overload trip
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage at the auxiliary/seal-in contact output terminal while the contactor is supposed to be held in. Should read the control supply voltage (24VDC). If it reads 0V while the coil is energized and the main contacts are closed, the auxiliary contact face is pitted, has a broken spring, or the contact block is mechanically loose — it's not actually closing despite the rest of the unit operating. A light touch on the side of the contact block often reproduces or clears the fault (classic intermittent).

failureModes.symptom: New replacement contactor installed, unit won't latch after the first press of Start — holds only while button is held, releases on button release
failureModes.catch: Verify the wiring to the auxiliary contact terminal. On a different-model replacement, the auxiliary contact block terminal numbering may differ from the old unit (e.g., old unit: terminals 13/14 for NO auxiliary; new unit: terminals 43/44). The seal-in wire landed on the wrong terminal — the contact it's connected to is an NC auxiliary or a non-existent terminal. Use the datasheet terminal diagram, not just positional matching.

warStory: We had a 30HP Maguire blender drive contactor on the FL3 line that would trip the overload about 45 seconds after every cold start — ran fine once it was warm. Maintenance kept replacing the overload relay assuming it was nuisance-tripping. I clamped all three legs at the motor while someone held Start: L1 and L2 were pulling 210% of nameplate, L3 was zero. Killed it, manually actuated the contactor by hand with a screwdriver on the operator button and resistance-checked all three power poles — poles 1 and 2 under half a milliohm, pole 3 at 47 ohms. The contact carrier on pole 3 had cracked and the bridge was floating, making just enough intermittent contact to pass a no-load bench test but collapsing under real motor inrush. The motor winding hadn't failed yet but the insulation on the phase-B coil group was already dark brown. Forty-five dollars for a new contactor body versus a rewind that would have been $1,800.
fieldNotes: A 3-phase voltage UNBALANCE of 1% causes approximately 6–10% current unbalance; a 3.5% voltage unbalance causes ~25% current unbalance and cuts motor service life by half (NEMA MG1-14.35).

Contactor inrush (pick-up) current on the coil is typically 6–10x the sealed (holding) current — a 24VDC coil drawing 60mA sealed may spike 400–600mA for the first 20–50ms on energize. Control wiring and fuses must handle this, or you get a coil that chatters on marginal control voltage.

AC-3 utilization category = motor switching duty (make against locked-rotor current, break against running current). AC-1 = resistive/slightly inductive. Never substitute AC-1 for AC-3 on motor circuits.

Auxiliary contact blocks are rated separately from main power poles — typically 10A AC-15 or 6A DC-13. The DC-13 rating matters for 24VDC seal-in circuits because DC arcs don't self-extinguish at a zero-crossing; always use a contact block rated for DC control if your control supply is DC.

quickCheck.symptom: You just installed a replacement contactor on a blender starter (old unit was end-of-life). You press Start: the coil clicks in, the motor spins up fine, all three legs read good voltage — but the instant you let go of the Start button, everything drops out. Hold Start continuously and the motor runs; release it and it stops. The old contactor never did this.

options.label: The new contactor's coil is weak and can't sustain a seal without help from the Start button
options.why: Wrong — the motor runs and stays running the whole time Start is held, which means the coil is pulling in fine and the main poles are closing fine. A weak coil would show up as chatter or failure to pull in at all, not a clean drop the instant Start releases.

options.label: The seal-in wire is landed on the wrong auxiliary terminal — the new contactor's aux block likely uses different terminal numbers than the old one, and the wire is sitting on an NC contact or an unused terminal instead of the NO seal-in contact
options.why: Correct. This is a known trap on contactor replacements: aux terminal numbering isn't standardized across models (e.g., old unit's NO aux on 13/14, new unit's on 43/44). If the seal-in wire lands on the wrong terminal, the holding path never actually closes, so the circuit only stays energized as long as the Start button itself supplies the path — exactly the symptom here. Check the new unit's datasheet terminal diagram, don't just match wire position to the old unit.

options.label: The motor's overload relay is auto-resetting every time it trips, which looks like a dropout
options.why: Wrong — an overload trip would normally latch open until manually reset or would show a trip flag/indicator, and it wouldn't correlate perfectly with releasing the Start button. The behavior tracks the button, not a thermal trip, which points at the control/seal-in circuit, not the overload.

title: Match the Control Voltage to the Coil Voltage
objective: Establish the hard rule that control voltage must match coil voltage, with the consequences of mismatching.
plainExplanation: Every relay coil is built for a specific voltage — 12VDC, 24VDC, 24VAC, 120VAC, and so on — and the voltage you feed it through your buttons and contacts MUST match it. In all our circuits everything was 24VDC, which is why it worked. Two classic warnings: feed a 12VDC coil with 120VAC and you 'let the smoke out' — it fries instantly. Feed a 120VAC coil with only 12VDC and no smoke, but it simply never pulls in — nothing happens, ever. Always read the coil's rating and match it.
interactiveElement: A coil-voltage matcher: pick the coil's rating, then pick the control voltage you apply. Correct match = relay clicks in; too-high = a smoke-puff animation and 'coil burned out'; too-low = a dud 'nothing happens' state.
checkQuestion: You apply 120VAC to a relay whose coil is rated 12VDC. What happens, and what's the rule?
checkAnswer: You let the smoke out — the coil burns up almost instantly. The rule: the control voltage through your buttons and contacts must MATCH the coil's rated voltage. Too high fries it; too low just never energizes it.
buildsOn: m15-poles-for-3phase (everything worked because voltages matched — now make that rule explicit, original print 12)
deepDive: A relay coil is a wound resistor with inductance. At 24VDC the coil resistance might be 200-400 ohms, drawing around 60-120mA steady-state — that's sealed current. But the instant you apply voltage the inductance hasn't built its back-EMF yet, so for the first 15-30 milliseconds the coil sees something closer to its wire resistance alone and inrush current can spike 6-10x the sealed value. This matters because it means a borderline-low control voltage that barely reads 18VDC under load may still pull the relay in — the inrush overcomes the weak field — but then the relay chatters as the coil can't hold against the spring at reduced sealed current. Every chatter cycle is an inrush spike. A coil rated 24VDC running at 18V long-term runs hotter than spec and chatters; 1000 chatter cycles is a burned coil, and the wire insulation fails first.

Overvoltage is faster and more obvious. Apply 120VAC to a 24VDC coil — which is roughly 170V peak — and you're pushing 5-7x the coil's rated power through that wire. P = V² / R; if the coil is 300 ohms and you apply 120VAC (use 120V RMS), power dissipated jumps from (24²/300) = 1.9W at rating to (120²/300) = 48W. The insulation vaporizes in seconds. You'll smell it before you see it. A 24VAC coil is almost the same resistance but built for the RMS heating curve of AC — feed it 24VDC and it also overheats because DC doesn't have the zero-crossings that give AC coils a fractional cooling opportunity in their thermal design. Some manufacturers compensate; many don't. Never assume interchangeability between 24VAC and 24VDC coils even if the number matches.

The dangerous middle ground is not the extreme cases — it's the 'almost right' voltage. A 120VAC coil fed from a sagging control transformer that puts out 108VAC at full load will pull in sluggishly and might chatter if the transformer dips further during motor starting. That same transformer might read 118VAC at the secondary terminals open-circuit but collapse to 98VAC when four coils try to energize simultaneously. A meter reading at the transformer secondary with nothing loaded tells you nothing — you need voltage AT the coil under load. Control-transformer undersizing is the number-one cause of unexplained coil chatter and relay drop-out in aging panels. The transformer's VA rating divided by the number of coils it feeds should give each coil at least 125% headroom.

The voltage-matching rule extends to contact ratings too, and people confuse them. The coil voltage is what energizes the relay — the control side. The contact voltage rating is what the contacts themselves can safely switch — the load side. A 24VDC coil relay can carry 250VAC through its contacts if the contact rating says so. Mixing these up in either direction creates trouble: running a 120VAC load through contacts rated only for 24VDC will arc and pit the contacts on every open cycle because the DC contact rating is always lower than the equivalent AC rating (AC extinguishes arcs at zero crossing; DC arcs have to be physically broken). The lesson: read BOTH voltage ratings off the datasheet, not just one.

failureModes.symptom: Relay pulls in sluggishly, audible buzz or chatter, contacts arc visibly at the tips, machine starts and immediately drops out or runs rough
failureModes.catch: Meter reads 118VAC open at transformer secondary, drops to 96VAC measured at coil terminals with all coils energized — under-VA transformer, confirm by checking transformer nameplate VA vs total coil VA demand

failureModes.symptom: Relay coil smokes or burns up within seconds of pressing Start, strong electrical-burn smell, relay dead on first energize
failureModes.catch: Wrong control voltage fed to coil — meter the supply rails in the panel BEFORE wiring a replacement; confirm coil voltage rating on the relay body (stamped or on label) matches what your meter reads between L1 and N of the control circuit

failureModes.symptom: Replacement relay installed, Start button pressed, nothing happens — no click, no pull-in, no smoke, no fault indication
failureModes.catch: Coil voltage is too low to overcome spring — meter reads 24VDC at coil terminals but relay is stamped 120VAC; the coil never sees enough ampere-turns to pull. Swap to correct voltage rating or rewire the control supply.

failureModes.symptom: Relay runs for weeks fine, then starts nuisance-dropping in the afternoon only — always comes back by morning
failureModes.catch: Thermal expansion on an undersized control transformer: measure voltage at the coil at 7 AM (reads 118VAC fine) versus 2 PM after eight hours of load cycling (reads 101VAC). The transformer VA is marginal and it heats up, increasing winding resistance and dropping output voltage below the coil's reliable pull-in threshold.

failureModes.symptom: New relay chatters constantly even though it's the 'correct' replacement part; the original ran fine for years
failureModes.catch: Check coil type: 24VAC vs 24VDC — visually identical relays, same footprint, one digit different on the label. Feeding a 24VDC coil from a 24VAC supply means the coil sees RMS heating without the inductive reactance it was designed for; it overheats and may chatter. Meter the supply to confirm AC vs DC before installing.

warStory: Midnight call on a packaging line — a brand-new panel was chattering on every relay in the section and two coils had already burned. The electrician who built it had wired all the control coils from the 24V terminals on the DC power supply, same as always, but the motor starters were 24VAC coils from a transformer — someone had ordered the wrong coil voltage when updating the BOM. Under no-load the meter at the power supply showed 24.3VDC and everything looked right. The second you energized one starter its coil saw DC through a coil designed for AC, heating rapidly; it buzzed at the inrush frequency and the insulation on two of them turned brown in under a minute. The fix was a $12 replacement coil and twenty minutes of tracing back which supply rail was actually feeding what — the whole crisis came down to 24VAC versus 24VDC on a part that looks identical until you read the label.
fieldNotes: A 24VDC coil and a 24VAC coil are NOT interchangeable — same voltage number, totally different coil design. Always check AC vs DC.
Coil inrush at energize is 6-10x sealed current for the first 15-30ms; a borderline-low voltage that pulls in may still chatter and burn the coil at sealed current.
Measure control voltage AT THE COIL TERMINALS under load — not at the transformer secondary open-circuit. A transformer that reads 118VAC open may collapse to 98VAC with four coils energized.

Contact voltage rating (what the contacts switch) is separate from coil voltage rating (what energizes the relay). A relay can have a 24VDC coil and 250VAC/10A contacts — read both specs off the datasheet.

quickCheck.symptom: A replacement 120VAC contactor hums loudly but won't pull in. You measure 118V at the coil terminals with the contactor disconnected, but it drops to 85V when connected.

options.label: The new contactor coil is shorted.
options.why: A shorted coil would likely blow a fuse or trip a breaker immediately, not just hum and cause a voltage drop.

options.label: The control transformer is undersized for the coil's inrush current.
options.why: This is the classic sign of transformer sag. The open-circuit voltage is fine, but it can't supply the required power under load, causing severe undervoltage.

options.label: The control wiring is too small for the distance.
options.why: While undersized wire causes voltage drop, this severe of a drop (over 30V) points more strongly to the source (transformer) being inadequate for the load.

title: Identify a Relay by Counting Its Pins
objective: Use the pin-counting rules (2 for coil, 1 per common, 1 or 2 per output) to identify relay type from terminals alone.
plainExplanation: You can often tell a relay's type just by counting its pins. The rules: always 2 pins for the coil; always 1 pin for each pole's COMMON; and 1 pin (single-throw) or 2 pins (double-throw) for each pole's output. So SPST = 4 pins, SPDT = 5, DPST = 6, DPDT = 8, 3PDT = 11, 4PDT = 14. Example: you find an unmarked relay with 8 pins — 2 are the coil, leaving 6 for the poles, which is 2 commons + 4 outputs = two double-throw poles = DPDT. (Coil voltage you still have to read off the part; there are exceptions, but this is the general rule.)
interactiveElement: A pin-counting puzzle: a relay base shows a set of pins; learner clicks to tag coil pins, commons, and outputs, then the app checks their math and names the relay (SPST...4PDT). Several randomized relays to identify.
checkQuestion: You find a relay with 5 pins. Using the pin-counting rules, what type is it?
checkAnswer: SPDT. Two pins are the coil, leaving three: one common plus two outputs = a single pole that's double-throw. (2 coil + 3 pole = 5 pins.)
buildsOn: m16-match-coil-voltage (now translate pole/throw counts into real pins on the part — original print 13)
deepDive: The pin-count rule works because relay manufacturers follow the same terminal arithmetic regardless of brand. Two pins always go to the coil — no exceptions in general-purpose relays. After that, every pole contributes a minimum of two pins: one for the common input and one for the single-throw output (SPST-style pole), or three pins for a double-throw pole (common plus NC plus NO). So a DPDT accounts for 2 coil + 2 commons + 4 outputs = 8 pins total, and every one of those is load-bearing. Miscounting even one pin costs you a return trip to the supply house or a miswired socket that fries the replacement the moment you power up.

Where this bites you on real hardware: an 11-pin 3PDT looks exactly like a 14-pin 4PDT relay from six feet away in a dusty panel, and both physically plug into sockets that are also similar. The difference is three pins. If you pull a 3PDT and drop in a 14-pin 4PDT because 'it's the same relay, different vendor,' the extra pole's NO and NC pins will be floating or shorted against adjacent socket strips depending on the base wiring. Result: phantom energization of a circuit that shouldn't be live, or a missing leg on a motor that now runs single-phase and burns up in 20 minutes. Count the pins before you pull the relay, not after.

The pin layout also tells you which terminal is the common versus the output, and this matters for continuity testing. With the relay de-energized, measuring across any pole you should see near-zero ohms (continuity) between the common and the NC pin, and open circuit between the common and NO pin. With the coil energized from its rated voltage, those readings flip. If you energize the coil at rated voltage and the NC-to-common still reads continuity, the contact is welded closed — common failure on contacts that switch inductive loads without snubber protection. A welded NC contact on a motor-reversing circuit is how machines start themselves uninvited. Coil inrush on a 24VDC relay typically runs 150–250 mA for 10–50 ms before dropping to a sealed current of 30–60 mA; that surge across a worn contact causes micro-welding over hundreds of cycles until the contact never opens again.

Industrial relays are packaged so the coil-pin spacing and pole-pin spacing are mechanically incompatible with each other — you physically cannot plug a coil wire into a contact pin on most 8-pin and 11-pin octal/ice-cube sockets. But with the less common screw-terminal or din-rail relay bases, nothing stops you from wiring L1 to coil pin A1 and neutral to A2 while accidentally reversing the pole connections. The consequnece is a coil that never sees rated voltage and chatters, while the load pins receive partial control voltage and the coil burns from the sustained inrush. Always cross-reference pin identification against the diagram stamped or printed on the relay body itself — every reputable relay ships with a wiring diagram on the top face. That diagram is your ground truth when the markings on the socket base are worn off or the previous tech used a marker to label things wrong.

failureModes.symptom: Replacement relay plugged in, control circuit powers up, but nothing on the load side ever actuates — coil hums or is silent, contacts don't switch
failureModes.catch: Ohmmeter across coil pins A1-A2 with relay out of socket: rated coil resistance is printed on the label (typically 400–1200 ohms for 24VDC relays). A reading of OL (open) means the coil is burned. Zero ohms means a shorted coil. Either way the relay is bad — but more likely you grabbed an 8-pin where a 11-pin was needed and the coil pins didn't align to the socket's power feed at all. Count pins again and verify pinout against the body diagram.

failureModes.symptom: Load stays energized after the Stop button is pressed and the coil is confirmed de-energized (coil voltage reads 0V)
failureModes.catch: With relay in socket and coil de-energized: measure resistance common-to-NC on each pole. Any pole reading near zero ohms when it should be the open NC-to-NO path has a welded contact. Pull the relay and actuate it manually — if the bar moves freely but continuity stays, it is a welded contact face. This is a mechanical failure, not a wiring error. Replace the relay; do NOT try to pry a welded contact open.

failureModes.symptom: Newly installed relay chatters rhythmically at 5–15 Hz, contactor sounds like a machine gun, load flickers
failureModes.catch: Measure AC volts across the coil pins IN the socket while it chatters. For a 24VDC coil reading should be stable DC; if you see 24VAC ripple or a voltage that collapses below 18VDC under load, the coil is not seeing clean DC. Check power supply output under load. Separately: if you installed a relay with more poles than the original, the extra poles may be switching a parallel path that is feeding back into the control circuit, causing oscillation. Verify no unintended pin contacts against the base.

failureModes.symptom: Relay pulls in correctly but one load never actuates while the others do — confirmed multi-pole relay
failureModes.catch: With relay energized, measure voltage common-to-output on each pole. The dead pole will read 0V output with correct voltage on common — meaning the contact face on that pole is open-circuit (pitted or mechanically failed on just that pole). An ohmmeter check with relay de-energized will confirm: NC reads open on the dead pole when it should read continuity. A pole-by-pole check is the only way to catch a single-pole failure inside a multi-pole relay.

failureModes.symptom: Relay base socket has burn marks around two adjacent pins; replacement relay also burns up within a day
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage between those two pins with BOTH relay and load disconnected. Any reading other than zero means a sneak voltage path exists — either adjacent socket strips are bridged by carbonized insulation, or the previous wiring error forced current through the wrong pins and tracked across the phenolic base. Ohmmeter between burned pins with everything de-energized: less than 1 megohm means the base is conducting and must be replaced before any new relay is installed.

warStory: A packaging line wouldn't stay running after a bearing-overload trip — maintenance swapped the contactor and it still tripped immediately. Third shift called me in at 2 AM, and the first thing I did was pull the relay out of the control socket and count: 11 pins, but the burned relay next to it in the parts cabinet was 8-pin. Someone had grabbed an 11-pin 3PDT off the shelf because 'it's the same relay, just bigger,' shoved it into an 8-pin octal base, and the third pole's pins were resting bare against the back of the cabinet panel — one of them was grounding directly to the enclosure, putting a dead short on the control circuit the instant the relay energized. The 'overload trip' wasn't a mechanical overload at all; it was a ground fault on the control side resetting the safety relay upstream. Sixty seconds with an ohmmeter from each pin to chassis before touching anything else, and the whole story was right there.
fieldNotes: Pin arithmetic: 2 (coil) + poles × (1 common + throws) = total pins. SPST=4, SPDT=5, DPST=6, DPDT=8, 3PDT=11, 4PDT=14.
24VDC relay coil resistance: typically 400–1200 ohms. Read it with an ohmmeter before installing — OL means burned open, near-zero means shorted coil.

With relay de-energized: common-to-NC should read continuity (near 0 ohms), common-to-NO should read open. Energize coil at rated voltage and those readings must flip — if they don't, the contact is welded.
The wiring diagram printed or molded onto the relay body is the ground truth for pin identification — never rely on socket markings alone, especially on a used or relabeled base.

quickCheck.symptom: Maintenance replaced a relay. Now, the machine is dead and the 24VDC power supply for the entire control panel has its fault light on.

options.label: The new relay's coil burned out and failed open.
options.why: An open coil would prevent the relay from working, but it would not short out and shut down the entire power supply.

options.label: An 11-pin relay was forced into an 8-pin socket, shorting +24V to 0V.
options.why: This is a common, catastrophic failure where unused pins on the wrong relay bridge the power supply terminals in the socket, creating a direct short.

options.label: The power supply coincidentally failed at the same time.
options.why: While possible, troubleshooting principle dictates you investigate the most recent change. The relay replacement is the probable cause.

title: Why a Plant Has Many Voltages (480/240/120/24)
objective: Frame the section-break question: why convert one incoming voltage into a whole ladder of voltages.
plainExplanation: Here's the 'why' behind all those coil and control voltages. A plant takes in one big voltage and steps it down through transformers and a power supply into a ladder: 480VAC for heavy motors, 240VAC for heaters and welders, 120VAC for legacy controls, and 24VAC/24VDC for modern sensors and logic. Each tier exists because no single voltage is good at every job at once — high voltage moves big power efficiently, low voltage is safe and cheap to wire. The next modules walk down that ladder and show the trade-off at each rung.
interactiveElement: An interactive one-line diagram: incoming 480VAC feeds transformers that branch to 240 / 120 / 24VAC and a power supply to 24VDC. Hovering each rung previews 'what runs here' (motors, heaters, controls, sensors).
checkQuestion: Why doesn't a plant just pick one voltage and use it for everything?
checkAnswer: Because no single voltage is good at every job. High voltage (480V) moves big power efficiently but is dangerous and impractical to fuse for small loads; low voltage (24V) is safe and cheap for controls but needs huge wire for big power. So we step one incoming voltage down into a ladder, matching each tier to its task.
buildsOn: m17-identify-by-pin-count (we kept meeting different coil voltages — here's the system behind them, original prints 14-15)
deepDive: The voltage hierarchy exists because physics won't let you cheat. Power (watts) is voltage times current — P = V × I. To move the same 50 kilowatts of power at 480V you only need 104 amps; at 120V you need 417 amps. Wire that carries 417A costs a fortune, weighs hundreds of pounds per hundred feet, and every connection is a fire hazard. So the plant takes in 480VAC three-phase at the service entrance and keeps it there for motors and heavy loads, then uses transformers to step it down for everything else. The transformer is lossless enough that stepping 480V down to 120V costs pennies in heat, but running 120V feeders across a plant for a motor would cost thousands in copper and conduit.

The 120VAC control tier — the one you still find on machines built before about 1995 — survives on inertia more than merit. The real problem: a meter reading 120VAC open-circuit means nothing when a coil pulls in. Control transformers are typically rated 50VA to 500VA, and a single 120VAC relay coil hits 6-10× its sealed current during inrush — maybe 50 milliamps sealed but 400 milliamps for the first 20-50 milliseconds. Wire a long run to that coil (say, 200 feet of #18 through a cable tray), add transformer impedance, and that inrush event collapses the coil terminal voltage from 120V to 80V or lower. Below about 85% of rated voltage the coil can't fully seat the armature. So it chatters: pulls partway in, loses enough voltage to drop out, recovers, pulls again — 10-20 times per second. Three days of chatter and the coil winding insulation is carbonized from I²R heat and the contacts are pitted from arcing under load. The machine didn't fail because of a 'bad relay.' It failed because of voltage drop under load on a 120VAC circuit that looked fine at 121.5V with your meter leads floating.

The move to 24VDC in the 1980s-90s wasn't just about safety — it solved the transformer-impedance problem by brute physics. A 24VDC power supply is a regulated switcher: it holds 24V ± 0.5V regardless of load transients, no inrush collapse, no chatter window. Coil current at 24V for the same relay is roughly 5× smaller than at 120VAC, so wire losses are 25× smaller (losses scale as I²R). That same 200-foot #18 run that caused 40V of drop at 120V inrush causes less than 2V of drop at 24VDC. NFPA 70E puts the safe extra-low-voltage boundary at 50V DC — anything under that doesn't require arc-flash PPE for control-circuit work, which cuts downtime every time you're tracing wiring live. A 24VDC control system also interfaces directly to PLCs and sensors with no transformer, which eliminates another failure point. You still need LOTO before touching the power side, but the control side becomes genuinely touch-safe.

Where the voltage tiers get dangerous in the field is at the handoff boundaries — the transformer and the power supply. A control transformer with a shorted secondary turn may still output 117VAC at no-load; it only reveals itself when a coil pulls in and terminal voltage collapses to 60V. The transformer isn't protecting the circuit from its own fault. Similarly, a 24VDC power supply with a failing output filter capacitor may read 24.1V on a DMM but show 3-4V of ripple on a scope — enough to make solid-state sensors false-trip at 30 Hz. Neither fault looks like a voltage-hierarchy problem until you've been burned by it. The lesson: whenever a relay chatters or a sensor flickers without a clear cause, grab a meter that can measure AC millivolts and put it on your 'DC' rail. If you see more than a few hundred millivolts of AC riding the DC, the power supply is failing, not the relay or sensor.

failureModes.symptom: Relay chatters rapidly on closure — a buzzing or rapid clicking instead of a solid pull-in; machine either won't run or runs rough
failureModes.catch: Measure AC voltage at the coil terminals WITH the coil attempting to pull in (not open-circuit). A reading below 85% of coil rating (e.g., below 102V on a 120VAC coil) during inrush confirms voltage-drop collapse. Check control transformer VA rating — a 50VA transformer feeding four coils is overloaded. At 24VDC systems, measure with AC millivolt function — more than 500mV AC on the DC rail points to a failing supply capacitor.

failureModes.symptom: Control circuit works fine at startup but fails intermittently after the plant has been running an hour — coil drops out randomly, seal-in breaks, machine stops without the Stop button being pressed
failureModes.catch: The control transformer is thermally marginal. Measure output voltage cold (plant just started) vs hot (after 1 hour of operation). A transformer dropping from 122V cold to 103V hot while carrying its load is near-failing. Measure secondary voltage with all loads connected, not with loads off — the difference reveals transformer impedance and VA headroom.

failureModes.symptom: A 24VDC sensor false-trips or flickers at a specific time of day, always when a large motor starts nearby — sensor output pulses low momentarily with no mechanical cause
failureModes.catch: The 24VDC rail sags under inrush of the nearby motor's power supply. Measure 24VDC supply output voltage at the moment of motor start. A sag below 20V for more than 10ms will false-trigger most NPN sensors. Fix: separate power supply for the sensor loop, or add a 1000µF bulk cap across the supply terminals.

failureModes.symptom: New relay installed to replace a failed one; coil hums loudly but relay doesn't pull in, contacts don't move
failureModes.catch: Voltage-tier mismatch — a 120VAC coil was installed where a 24VAC or 24VDC coil is required. Measure coil terminal voltage: if it reads 24V but the coil is rated 120V, it can't develop enough magnetic force. Check the failed relay's coil voltage marking and the replacement part number. 120VAC coils and 24VDC coils in the same form factor base are common warehouse confusion.

failureModes.symptom: Brand-new machine smells like burning plastic within the first week of operation; a relay coil is hot to the touch, wiring is discolored near that coil
failureModes.catch: Applied voltage is too high — a 24VDC coil on a 120VAC circuit, or a 120VAC coil on a 240VAC line. Measure voltage at the coil terminals before pulling the failed part. Excess voltage means excess current in direct proportion (V=IR), and coil wire gauge is sized for rated voltage only — overvoltage drives I²R heat that the insulation cannot shed.

warStory: A packaging line in a pet food plant kept dropping out its infeed conveyor every afternoon around 2 PM — never in the morning, never at night, always mid-shift. The PLC showed a 'coil-open fault' on the control relay. Three relays in two weeks, all 'bad.' Turned out the 120VAC control transformer was a 100VA unit feeding eight relay coils on a circuit that had grown over ten years. By 2 PM the transformer was heat-soaked and its secondary had sagged from 121V cold to 97V hot under full load — just enough to keep the coils chattering until one coil's winding insulation failed from the heat. Swapping in a 250VA transformer cost $40 and the fault never came back. The relays weren't bad; they were starving for voltage that the nameplate said was there.
fieldNotes: 85% of rated coil voltage is the absolute minimum for reliable pull-in — below that the armature won't fully seat and chatter begins. For a 120VAC coil that's 102V; for a 24VDC coil that's 20.4V.

Control transformer VA rule of thumb: add up the sealed VA of every coil on the secondary, then multiply by 1.5 to cover inrush stacking when multiple coils pull in simultaneously. A transformer that's sized for sealed current will sag badly under inrush.

NFPA 70E defines 50V DC (and 25V AC) as the safe extra-low voltage threshold — below 50VDC, bare-hand contact with control wiring is generally permitted without arc-flash PPE. This is a primary driver for 24VDC control systems in new designs.

Coil inrush current is typically 6-10× sealed current for 120VAC relay coils. A coil that draws 40mA sealed may hit 300-400mA for the first 20-50ms on energization — size your control transformer and wire accordingly or voltage drop will cause chatter.

quickCheck.symptom: Every time a large 120VAC contactor pulls in, a 24VDC sensor on a long wire run momentarily drops out, faulting the PLC.

options.label: The sensor is picking up electrical noise (EMI) from the contactor.
options.why: While EMI is a possibility, a momentary complete dropout is more characteristic of a power issue than induced noise.

options.label: The single control transformer feeding everything is sagging under the inrush.
options.why: The contactor's high inrush current is causing the transformer's 120VAC output to sag, which in turn causes the 24VDC power supply fed by it to momentarily dip below the sensor's operating voltage.

options.label: The PLC input card is faulty.
options.why: If the PLC input sees the signal disappear, it's doing its job. The problem lies with why the signal is disappearing from the sensor.

title: The Fusing Math: Why High Voltage Is Hard to Protect
objective: Apply Amps = Watts / Volts to show why high-voltage small loads are nearly impossible to fuse, driving the move to lower voltages.
plainExplanation: There's a formula behind the whole voltage ladder: Amps = Watts ÷ Volts. Take a small 100-watt load: at 480V it draws only 100 ÷ 480 ≈ 0.21A, so you'd need a 480V fuse that blows at a quarter-amp — those barely exist. The same 100W at 240V is ~0.42A (still nuisance-trip territory if voltage dips), and a small dip raising the amp draw makes it worse. Drop to 24V and currents are big and easy to fuse with common cheap fuses. That fusing pain is a major reason industry moved control circuits down to 120V and especially 24V.
interactiveElement: A calculator slider: set Watts and Volts, watch Amps update live via A = W / V, with a 'fuse availability' meter that goes red at the tiny high-voltage amp values and green at the easy low-voltage ones.
checkQuestion: A 100W load runs at 480V. Roughly how many amps does it draw, and why is that a fusing problem?
checkAnswer: About 0.21A (100 ÷ 480). A fuse that interrupts 480V yet blows at only a quarter-amp is almost impossible to find — so protecting small high-voltage loads is impractical. Lower voltages give larger, easy-to-fuse currents.
buildsOn: m18-why-a-voltage-hierarchy (put numbers on the ladder — original prints 15-16's fuse problem)
deepDive: The formula Amps = Watts / Volts is not abstract textbook math — it is the exact reason your control cabinet looks the way it does. A 100-watt solenoid valve or indicator transformer at 480VAC draws 100 / 480 = 0.208A. That 0.208A is the current the fuse must carry during normal operation and interrupt on a fault. The problem: fuses rated for 480VAC interrupting capacity start in the 1A range for most industrial fuse families. A 1A fuse on a 0.208A load means the fuse runs at roughly 20% of its rated trip point — any voltage dip or inrush spike and you're in nuisance-trip territory, but more importantly the fuse provides almost no granular protection. The load could draw 0.6A (3x normal, a genuine fault) and that same 1A fuse doesn't blink.

Drop to 120VAC and the same 100W load becomes 100 / 120 = 0.83A. Now a 1A fuse is actually meaningful — it will trip on roughly 1.2x-1.5x overcurrent because the fuse is working near its rating. Drop further to 24VDC and 100W = 4.17A. A 5A fuse protects that circuit tightly and a blown fuse is clearly audible and visually obvious in a fuse-block. This is why every modern control panel runs 24VDC logic on 2A or 4A branch fuses: the math gives you numbers that real, cheap, off-the-shelf fuses were designed for. At 480V those same common fuse sizes represent hundreds of watts of unchecked fault current — completely wrong for a 100W device.

There is also a heating side to this math nobody tells beginners. Power dissipated in a wire is P = I² × R (current squared times resistance). At 480V the 0.208A through 100 feet of 18-gauge wire (R ≈ 0.64 Ω) dumps only 0.028W of heat — nearly nothing, which looks like a win. But when that circuit faults and draws, say, 2A before the fuse reacts, the wire heat jumps to 2² × 0.64 = 2.56W per 100 feet, still low. The hidden danger is the fuse NEVER reacts fast enough at these tiny currents because you cannot spec a fuse tight enough without chronic nuisance trips. The fault current drifts up slowly, the insulation roasts over minutes, and by the time someone notices the ammeter is climbing, you've got a smoldering wire inside conduit. Protecting small loads at high voltage means you are trading fuse precision for the convenience of using one voltage everywhere — a trade that has caused more than a few midnight panel fires.

Coil inrush matters here too. When a relay or solenoid first pulls in, the inrush current can be 6-10x the sealed (holding) current for the first 30-100 milliseconds. A contactor coil rated 25VA sealed may pull 150-200VA at inrush. At 24VDC: 25VA sealed = 1.04A, inrush spike reaches 8A for 50ms. A 2A fast-blow fuse trips on that — you use a time-delay (Slo-Blo) fuse instead, which rides through the inrush but still catches a sustained overcurrent. At 480VAC: sealed VA might be 40VA = 0.083A, inrush = 0.5A for 50ms. The time-delay fuse you'd pick is 1A, which gives you almost zero headroom between normal inrush and rated trip. Nuisance trips on startup are almost guaranteed. Move to 120V and 24V and suddenly the inrush numbers land in ranges that standard time-delay fuse curves were actually engineered for.

failureModes.symptom: Small solenoid or indicator lamp on a 480VAC branch keeps blowing its fuse on every startup attempt, even though the device itself is fine
failureModes.catch: Calculate expected current: if the load VA divided by 480 gives you less than 0.5A, you are below the minimum useful fuse rating. Measure inrush with a clamp meter set to peak-capture — if it spikes above the fuse rating for even 50ms you've found the nuisance-trip culprit. Solution is usually relocating the load to a 120V or 24VDC transformer secondary where fuse selection is practical.

failureModes.symptom: Control circuit fuse runs warm or discolors at the fuse block over weeks without tripping, eventually the panel smells like hot plastic
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage drop across the fuse under load (with the circuit running). More than 0.1V across a fuse means the fuse element or ferrule contact is degraded and carrying resistive heat — P = I²R in the fuse itself. Pull and inspect: a good fuse shows clean ferrules; a failing one shows oxidation or slight arc pitting. Torque specs on the fuse-block clamp screws matter — spec is typically 20-25 in-lb for a 30A block.

failureModes.symptom: A 24VDC control circuit reads correct voltage at the power supply output but a device two panel sections away sees only 18-19VDC under load
failureModes.catch: Amps = Watts / Volts backward: if a 10W solenoid draws 0.42A at 24V and your 22-gauge home-run wire has 1.5 Ω resistance over 200 feet of run, Ohm's law gives V-drop = 0.42 × 1.5 = 0.63V each direction = 1.26V total drop. Stack several of these loads on one run and the supply rail under load is 20V at the far end. Fix: upsize wire or add a distributed 24VDC supply closer to the field devices.

failureModes.symptom: A relay coil rated 24VDC chatters and buzzes when its circuit is energized even though the supply reads 24V at the power supply terminals
failureModes.catch: Measure 24VDC at the coil terminals while the relay is attempting to pull in. If it collapses from 24V to 15-18V under inrush load, your power supply is undersized or current-limiting. Inrush for that coil may be 5-8x sealed current for 50-100ms — the supply must be rated above the peak inrush of all coils that might close simultaneously, not just the sum of sealed currents. Spec note: size 24VDC supplies at 150% of calculated sealed-current load to absorb simultaneous inrush.

warStory: I was called to a 1980s extrusion line that kept tripping a 30A branch fuse on the 480V cabinet, always on second shift, always at startup. The fuse was a 30A protecting a single 2.5kW heater band — no problem there, the math worked fine. But buried in the same fuse block someone had tapped a 480V control feed for six indicator lights and a solenoid valve, total load maybe 120 watts = 0.25A. The 30A fuse would never protect that 0.25A wiring. On a cold startup the heater inrush and the solenoid inrush hit simultaneously, the 30A fuse rode through both, but the 18-gauge wire feeding the indicators — completely unprotected for its actual current rating — had been charring its insulation jacket inside the wireway for two years. The real fix was not a bigger fuse; it was pulling those small 480V loads off and wiring them from the 120V transformer secondary sitting three feet away where a 2A fuse actually meant something.
fieldNotes: Amps = Watts / Volts (rearranged: Watts = Volts × Amps, Volts = Watts / Amps) — memorize the triangle, it is on every fusing and wire-sizing decision you will ever make.

Practical fuse minimum for reliable protection: your load current should be at least 30-40% of the fuse's trip rating. If the math gives you less than that, you are on the wrong voltage tier for that load.
Coil inrush is typically 6-10x sealed (holding) current for 50-150ms — always use time-delay (Slo-Blo / Type T) fuses on coil circuits, never fast-blow.
