Look for blue-black discoloration at contact faces. Do NOT just replace the coil — the contacts must be replaced or the entire contactor swapped.

failureModes.symptom: Overload trips every day at approximately the same time, always after the machine has been running 30–60 minutes
failureModes.catch: Check contact resistance across main contacts under load (requires a clamp meter on each phase leg during run). Measure all three phases — if one phase reads notably higher current than the others, it has high-resistance pitted contacts heating the OL bimetal unevenly. At rest the contact reads low resistance; load it and the resistance increases with temperature (it's a degraded contact, not a dead one).

failureModes.symptom: Machine is completely dead — no hum, no click, no indicator lights — but the incoming supply voltage checks out fine
failureModes.catch: Measure across the control fuse (line-to-line on both fuse terminals) with control power on: a good fuse reads 0V; a blown fuse reads full supply voltage (24V or 120V) because you're measuring across an open element in a series circuit. This is the one reading that confuses new techs — 24V across a fuse means it's blown, not good.

warStory: We had a press-room conveyor at an extrusion plant that would run fine all morning and then drop out randomly, usually once or twice after lunch — never during the morning shift, never captured on the meter. Three guys chased it for two weeks: new Stop button, new seal-in contact, new relay, new start/stop station. Still dropped out. I sat on the machine on a Friday afternoon with a clamp meter on the 24VDC supply and a temperature gun. The drop-out finally happened at 1:47 PM: control voltage dipped to 13.8V for about 200ms and the coil released. The root cause was a control transformer secondary terminal that had been loose for years — loose enough to arc microscopically when it heated up after a few hours under load, dropping resistance, then voltage, just long enough to release the coil. Torqued the terminal to spec, hit it with dielectric grease, never saw the fault again. Four weeks of parts-swapping solved by a torque driver and a thermometer.
fieldNotes: A GOOD fuse reads ~0V across its terminals; a BLOWN fuse reads full supply voltage. This is the opposite of what new techs expect — memorize it.
Relay coil inrush current is typically 6–10x the sealed (holding) current. A chattering relay is stuck in continuous inrush — it will burn the coil in minutes, not hours.

Minimum switching current is a real spec: a contact rated 10A 250VAC may require 100mA minimum to self-wipe. Low-current 24VDC seal-in contacts in aging relays oxide over and fail open — use gold-plated contacts or relays rated for low-level switching.

Welded contacts and a dropped-out coil sound the same from the control side (the coil click is gone on Stop). The proof is across the open main contacts with power off: good = open circuit, welded = 0–2 ohms.

quickCheck.symptom: You clamp-meter all three phases on a running contactor. Two legs read normal current, but one leg runs noticeably hotter to the touch and the overload trips almost every day, always 30-60 minutes into the run — never right at startup. At rest, an ohmmeter across that phase's contact reads nearly the same low resistance as the other two.

options.label: Replace the overload relay — it's obviously miscalibrated if it only trips on one phase
options.why: The overload is doing its job correctly by tripping on a real imbalance — replacing it just delays the same trip. The pattern (one hot leg, fine at cold/rest ohms) points at the contact itself, not the overload's calibration.

options.label: That phase's contact is pitted — resistance is fine at rest (peaks still touching) but rises under load as the craters heat up, unevenly loading the OL bimetal until it trips
options.why: Pitted (not welded) contacts often read normal resistance cold because the remaining high points still touch, but under real current and heat the degraded surface area drives resistance and heat up on just that leg — exactly the daily-after-30-60-min pattern. Welded contacts wouldn't allow the overload to open at all, and a bad OL calibration wouldn't single out one phase's heat.

options.label: The motor winding on that phase is shorted and needs to be rewound
options.why: A shorted motor winding would show up as an electrical imbalance from the motor side and usually trips much faster and more consistently (often at start), and megger/winding tests would catch it — the contact-side clue (cold ohms fine, hot under load, one leg only) points upstream at the contactor's pitted contact, not the motor.

title: Capstone: Diagnose a Dead Motor Start/Stop Station
objective: Independently diagnose a non-running motor circuit end-to-end, applying LOTO, the method, the meter, ladder reading, and fault knowledge.
plainExplanation: Time to put it all together with no hand-holding. You're handed a real scenario: an operator reports the conveyor motor won't start when they press Start. You'll work it like a pro — read the ladder print to know normal, apply LOTO and verify-dead where needed, then methodically divide the circuit and measure: is control voltage present, does the coil energize, does the seal-in hold, is an overload tripped, is a fuse blown. You'll localize the fault to a single component, name it, and state the fix — exactly what the job asks of you every day.
interactiveElement: An open-ended capstone simulator: a fully wired start/stop motor circuit with a hidden injected fault and a ladder print. The learner takes free actions (LOTO steps, meter readings at any point, inspect components) and must submit a diagnosis + fix; the app scores method, safety, and correctness, then replays the ideal path.
checkQuestion: You press Start and nothing happens; you measure 24VDC present at the coil terminals but the contactor doesn't pull in. What's your conclusion?
checkAnswer: The control circuit is doing its job — correct voltage is reaching the coil — so the fault is the contactor/coil itself (open or burned-out coil) or a mechanical jam. Because voltage is present and matched but it won't energize, replace the coil/contactor; the upstream control wiring and buttons are proven good.
buildsOn: m27-common-real-faults + m28-safety-loto (synthesize the entire course into one real, safety-first diagnosis)
deepDive: The half-split method is not optional on ANY of these tickets — whether the symptom is 'won't start,' 'won't stop,' a stalled interlock, or a lying pilot light, the discipline is the same: pick a test point roughly in the MIDDLE of the suspect circuit, take one measurement, and let that single reading eliminate half of what's left. On a no-start call, that midpoint is usually the coil terminals — voltage present but no pull-in narrows it to the coil itself or downstream; 0V sends you working backward through Stop, Start, seal-in, and the fuse. On a won't-stop call, the same idea runs the other direction — measure the coil for voltage AFTER Stop is pressed; power still there means the control circuit failed to drop, no power with the load still running means a welded main contact downstream of the coil entirely. Whatever the ticket, resist the urge to start at one end and walk the whole rung; a well-placed middle measurement cuts your search in half every time.

Voltage readings alone can lie. A meter probing an open terminal can read phantom voltage induced from adjacent conductors running in the same conduit — 20 to 40 VAC on what should be a dead 24VDC wire, or 60V AC on a line you cut. Always use a low-impedance meter (CAT III, Z-check or LoZ function) to kill the ghost reading. High-impedance digital meters are excellent for precision but terrible at spotting induced voltage — they'll read 80V on a completely open conductor if it runs parallel to a live 480V line long enough. Before you declare a circuit dead, put the LoZ function on it and watch the number collapse to under 5V if the voltage wasn't real. This is particularly brutal on motor starter panels where control wiring and power wiring share conduit for 50 feet. That phantom 40VAC reading has caused more than one technician to spend an hour hunting a 'bad Stop button' that was energized the whole time.

Contact failure modes are specific and each has its own fingerprint. A pitted contact pair on a contactor — carbon craters from repeated arcing — will pass voltage in the lab with light spring pressure but collapse under load. You'll see the motor try to start (growl, hum, maybe stall) and kill the overload. Test it: with the contactor pulled in, measure voltage drop across each main contact while the motor is loaded. Healthy contacts drop less than 0.5V. A pitted set might show 4–8V drop per contact; three phases of 6V drop each means 18V less reaching the motor, and the motor runs hot, slow, and trips overloads. Welded contacts are the opposite: the motor won't stop even after the Stop button kills the coil. Your proof is measuring continuity across an open contactor's main contacts with the coil de-energized — you'll get less than 1 ohm where you should see OL. Never assume 'the contactor dropped out' based on the pilot light alone. A welded contactor with a working seal-in shows a Running light even after Stop; the machine is physically still hot, and a welded 480V contact is a fire-and-injury event waiting for the next maintenance window.

The overload relay is the most skipped check in motor diagnostics and the source of probably 40% of 'dead motor' calls. On a standard combination motor starter, the overload (OL) device has an NC contact wired in series with the coil circuit — if the motor exceeded its full-load amperage rating long enough, that OL contact trips open and the coil cannot energize regardless of what your Start button does. Operators rarely notice the tripped OL indicator; they just report 'motor won't run.' Measure voltage ahead of the OL contact and behind it: full 24VDC before, 0V after = OL is tripped. Reset it and the circuit works instantly. But here's the failure trap: if you reset the OL without finding out why it tripped, you're setting the stage for burned windings. An OL trips because the motor drew too much current for too long — mechanical jam, low supply voltage collapsing under load, a shorted winding pulling extra current, or simply an OL rated too low for the application. Pull the motor current with a clamp meter on all three legs while it runs. Full-load amps nameplate is your number; anything more than 105% of FLA on any leg is a real problem that reset alone won't fix.

Misdiagnosis has a price tag, and it is not symmetric. Swapping a $200 contactor that turned out to be fine costs you an afternoon and some parts-budget embarrassment. Missing a pair of pitted, high-resistance contacts — the ones quietly running hot instead of failing clean — and calling the station “fixed” is how that same contactor cooks a $5,000 motor a week later on second shift, with nobody there to catch the smell before it lets go. The meter work in this capstone is not paperwork: skipping a measurement to save five minutes is how a cheap fix turns into a five-figure one, and how a repeat callback becomes a career-limiting conversation with your supervisor.

failureModes.symptom: Press Start, nothing happens at all — no click, no hum, no movement on any indicator
failureModes.catch: Measure 24VDC at the coil terminals. If 0V: trace back toward the control fuse — measure at fuse output (0V there = blown fuse, open on an OHM check across a de-energized fuse). If 24VDC at coil but no pull-in: coil resistance OHM check. A good 24VDC relay coil is typically 300–1000 ohms; OL (open) means burned coil.

failureModes.symptom: Motor starts when you hold Start but drops out the instant you release the button
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage at the seal-in contact terminals while the coil is energized by the Start button. Should read ~24VDC across the closed contact (near-zero V drop). A 24VDC reading where there should be 0V drop means the contact isn't closing — confirm with an in-circuit continuity check after de-energizing: sealing contact should be closed (under 1 ohm) when coil is pulled in.

failureModes.symptom: Overload trips within 30 seconds of starting every time, motor runs but hot and slow
failureModes.catch: Clamp-meter all three legs while running. Compare to nameplate FLA. Also measure voltage at each contactor output terminal to motor T1/T2/T3 with motor under load — a pitted or high-resistance contact will show 4–8V drop instead of <0.5V. Also check for single-phasing: if one leg reads zero, one winding is carrying all the work and will trip the OL in seconds.

failureModes.symptom: Motor won't stop after pressing Stop button — contactor stays pulled in
failureModes.catch: Measure voltage at the coil terminals after pressing Stop. Should immediately read 0V. If still 24VDC: welded contactor contacts or a sneak path keeping the coil powered (check for a parallel-wired contact or a stuck Start button with your meter across it). If 0V at coil but contactor still closed: welded main contacts — prove it by measuring ohms across the open main contacts with the coil de-energized. Should read OL; anything under 10 ohms is a weld.

failureModes.symptom: Motor runs fine cold but trips the overload after 15–20 minutes, every shift
failureModes.catch: First check nameplate FLA vs OL amp-setting (OL dial or trip-class amp range). Then clamp-meter all three legs at full warm load and check for leg imbalance greater than 5% — an unbalanced supply voltage of even 2% can cause 10–15% current unbalance on the high-current phase, cooking that winding. Measure L1/L2/L3 supply voltage at the motor disconnect: if one leg is 3+ volts low relative to the others under load, the problem is upstream of the panel, not the motor or overload.

warStory: We had a 200-horsepower extruder drive that would run fine all morning and then trip with 'no start' after the lunch break — every single day for two weeks. Maintenance had already replaced the Stop button, the contactor, and two OL relays. I finally sat down with a clamp logger on the 24VDC control circuit at the coil and watched: every day at 12:15 the control voltage dropped from 24.3VDC to 18.1VDC for about 90 seconds and the coil chattered itself out of the latch. Turned out a cleaning crew was plugging a 1,200-watt pressure washer into a duplex outlet that shared the control transformer secondary with the panel — voltage collapse under that unexpected load dropped it below the contactor's 85% dropout threshold. The 'motor fault' was a 15-amp duplex outlet across the hall.
fieldNotes: NEMA contactor pull-in voltage is typically 85–110% of rated coil voltage; below 85% and it chatters or won't seal. Above 110% and coil overheats. Measure the coil with the coil actually energized, not open-circuit — a weak control transformer can look fine at 24.5VDC open and collapse to 18VDC when the coil inrush hits (coil inrush can be 6–10x sealed current in the first 20–50ms).

Overload relay full-load amp setting must be set to motor nameplate FLA, not the breaker or wire size. Rule of thumb: if the motor nameplated FLA is 14A, set the OL to 14A — not 20 because that's the wire rating. Setting the OL too high is how motors burn silently for months.

Voltage drop across a CLOSED healthy contactor main contact under load: less than 0.5V per contact. More than 2V per contact means pitting or contamination — the contact is dissipating real wattage as heat, and the motor is seeing reduced voltage. Three contacts at 3V drop each = 9V robbed from the motor on a 480V system.

Live-dead-live on every verification: prove your meter reads correctly on a KNOWN live source, make your measurement, prove it still reads correctly. A meter with a dead battery or a broken probe lead reads 0V everywhere and has killed people who trusted a '0V = safe' reading without the bookend checks.

quickCheck.symptom: You get called to a bagging line conveyor. The operator says he pressed Stop five minutes ago but the motor is still turning and the Running pilot light is still lit. You walk up, LOTO isn't applied yet, and you need to know what you're dealing with before you touch anything.

options.label: Assume the Stop button is just wired wrong and start pulling the Start/Stop wiring apart to fix it
options.why: That skips the diagnostic step entirely and puts your hands into a starter that may still have a welded 480VAC main contact energizing the motor — exactly the fire-and-injury scenario a lit Running light after Stop should make you suspect first.

options.label: Measure voltage at the coil terminals right now, before touching anything else in the starter
options.why: This is the correct next diagnostic step: if the coil reads 0V but the motor is still running, the coil dropped out fine and the fault is welded main contacts downstream — a genuine hazard requiring LOTO and a continuity check across the open contacts (should read OL, not near-zero ohms). If the coil still reads 24VDC, the control circuit itself failed to drop, which is a different fault (sneak path or stuck Start button) with the same LOTO requirement before you touch the panel.

options.label: Trust the Running pilot light — since it's still lit, the contactor never dropped out, so just cycle Stop again a few more times
options.why: A pilot light proves nothing about the actual contactor state; a welded contactor with a working seal-in circuit can show a Running light even after the coil has fully de-energized. Repeating Stop presses wastes time and leaves a potentially welded 480V contact live and undiagnosed.
